args: The value whose absolute value you want
abs.description: Returns the absolute value of a number, making negatives positive while leaving positives unchanged. Use it whenever the sign doesn't matter and you only care about magnitude - error distances, deviations, or the difference between two values regardless of direction.

args: First interest date of the security
Annual coupon rate as a decimal
Face (par) value; defaults to 1000 if omitted
Number of coupon payments per year: 1=annual, 2=semiannual, 4=quarterly
accrint.description: Returns the accrued interest for a security that pays periodic interest. Use it when pricing bonds or other fixed-income instruments to determine how much interest has accumulated since the last coupon payment.
examples: =ACCRINT(issue, first_interest, settlement, rate, par, frequency, basis) returns the accrued interest.

args: Annual coupon rate as a decimal
Face (par) value; defaults to 1000 if omitted
accrintm.description: Returns the accrued interest on a security that pays interest at maturity rather than periodically. Use it when pricing zero-coupon or discount instruments where interest accumulates and is paid in full at the end.
examples: =ACCRINTM(issue, settlement, rate, par, basis) returns the total interest earned.

acos.description: Returns the arccosine (inverse cosine) of a number, in radians. Use it in geometry and trigonometry when you know the cosine of an angle and need to find the angle itself.

args: A number greater than or equal to 1
acosh.description: Returns the inverse hyperbolic cosine of a number. Used in advanced mathematics and physics for problems involving hyperbolic geometry or special functions.

acot.description: Returns the inverse cotangent of a number, in radians. A specialized trigonometric function used in surveying and engineering calculations.

args: A real number whose absolute value is greater than 1
acoth.description: Returns the inverse hyperbolic cotangent of a number. Appears in advanced mathematical and physical analysis.

args: Row number for the cell reference (1 = row 1)
Column number for the cell reference (1 = column A)
Reference type: 1=absolute, 2=row absolute/col relative, 3=row relative/col absolute, 4=relative
TRUE or omitted for A1 style; FALSE for R1C1 style
Sheet name to include in the address
address.description: Returns a cell address as a text string given a row number and column number. Use it to build dynamic cell references you can then pass to INDIRECT - for instance, constructing a reference from a formula-driven row and column.

and.description: Returns TRUE if all of its arguments evaluate to TRUE, FALSE if any is FALSE. Use it to combine multiple conditions in an IF formula - for example, flagging rows where both a sales target and a margin target are met.
examples: =AND(A2>100, B2>0.2) returns TRUE only when both conditions hold.

args: A string containing a Roman numeral, e.g. "XIV"
arabic.description: Converts a Roman numeral text string to a regular number. Use it when historical or legacy data uses Roman numerals and you need to perform arithmetic on the values.

args: A reference or multi-area selection whose area count you want
areas.description: Returns the number of separate areas (contiguous ranges) in a reference. In IronCalc we always return 1 as we do not support multi-area selections.

args: The array or range to convert to text
arraytotext.description: Converts an array or range to a text string, either in a concise display format or a strict parseable format. Use it when you want to show the contents of a spilled array as a single readable cell entry.

args: A string that may contain full-width characters
asc.description: Converts full-width (double-byte) characters to half-width (single-byte) equivalents in East Asian text. Use it to normalize text that may contain full-width characters entered via Japanese or Chinese input.

asin.description: Returns the arcsine (inverse sine) of a number, in radians. Use it in geometry when you know the sine of an angle and need the angle itself.

asinh.description: Returns the inverse hyperbolic sine of a number. Used in engineering and advanced mathematics where hyperbolic functions arise.

args: Any real number representing the tangent
atan.description: Returns the arctangent (inverse tangent) of a number in radians, in the range −π/2 to π/2. Use it to find an angle from a slope or ratio when the quadrant doesn't matter.

atan2.description: Returns the arctangent of a point defined by x and y coordinates in radians, correctly handling all four quadrants. Use it when you have x and y components and need the direction angle of a vector.

atanh.description: Returns the inverse hyperbolic tangent of a number. Used in advanced statistics (the Fisher z-transformation) and mathematics.

args: First value or range to include
Second value or range to include
avedev.description: Returns the average of the absolute deviations of data points from their mean - a simple, intuitive measure of spread. Use it to communicate variability to an audience that finds standard deviation abstract.

args: First value or range to average
average.description: Returns the arithmetic mean of a set of numbers.
examples: =AVERAGE(A2:A100) returns the mean of the range.

args: First value or range; text and FALSE count as 0, TRUE counts as 1
averagea.description: Like AVERAGE, but includes text (counted as 0) and logical values (TRUE=1, FALSE=0) in the calculation.

args: Cells to evaluate against the criteria
Cells to average; if omitted, averages the cells in range
averageif.description: Returns the average of cells that meet a single condition. Use it to find the mean for a specific category - for example, the average sale amount for a particular product.
examples: =AVERAGEIF(B2:B100, "Widgets", C2:C100) averages C where B is "Widgets".

args: First range to test against a condition
Additional criteria matching the extra ranges
averageifs.description: Returns the average of cells that meet multiple conditions simultaneously. The multi-condition version of AVERAGEIF - use it when you need to average a filtered subset with two or more criteria.
examples: =AVERAGEIFS(C2:C100, B2:B100, "West", D2:D100, "Q1") averages where both conditions are met.

args: Value to convert; must be a non-negative integer
Minimum character width of the result, padded with leading zeros
base.description: Converts a number to a text string representation in any base (radix) from 2 to 36. Use it as a general-purpose base converter - handy for bases not covered by DEC2BIN or DEC2HEX.

args: The value at which to evaluate the function
The order of the Bessel function, n
besseli.description: Returns the modified Bessel function of the first kind, Iₙ(x). Use it in science and engineering for problems involving heat conduction, signal processing, or cylindrical wave patterns.

args: The value at which to evaluate the function
The order of the Bessel function, n
besselj.description: Returns the Bessel function of the first kind, Jₙ(x). Used in physics and engineering - particularly for vibrating membranes, antenna design, and acoustics.

args: The value at which to evaluate the function
The order of the Bessel function, n
besselk.description: Returns the modified Bessel function of the second kind, Kₙ(x). Appears in engineering problems involving heat flow and diffusion in cylindrical geometries.

args: The value at which to evaluate the function
The order of the Bessel function, n
bessely.description: Returns the Bessel function of the second kind (Neumann function), Yₙ(x). Used alongside BESSELJ in wave and vibration problems where a second independent solution is required.

args: The value at which to evaluate the function
TRUE for cumulative distribution, FALSE for probability density function
Lower bound of the distribution; defaults to 0
Upper bound of the distribution; defaults to 1
beta.dist.description: Returns the beta distribution probability - either the cumulative probability (CDF) or the probability density (PDF) at a given value. Use it in project management and Bayesian statistics to model probabilities constrained between 0 and 1.
examples: =BETA.DIST(0.4, 2, 5, TRUE) returns the cumulative probability at 0.4.

args: Lower bound of the distribution; defaults to 0
Upper bound of the distribution; defaults to 1
beta.inv.description: Returns the inverse of the beta cumulative distribution - the value at which the CDF reaches a given probability. Use it to find percentile values of a beta-distributed variable.

args: Value to evaluate, between A and B
betadist.description: Compatibility name for BETA.DIST (cumulative form). Kept for compatibility with Excel 2007 and earlier workbooks. Prefer BETA.DIST in new formulas.

betainv.description: Compatibility name for BETA.INV. Kept for compatibility with Excel 2007 and earlier workbooks. Prefer BETA.INV in new formulas.

args: Binary string to convert, up to 10 digits
bin2dec.description: Converts a binary number (base 2) to its decimal equivalent. Use it when working with digital logic, bitwise operations, or any data that arrives in binary format.

args: Minimum width of the result, padded with leading zeros
bin2hex.description: Converts a binary number to hexadecimal. Use it when you need to move between binary and hex - common in programming and hardware documentation.

args: Minimum width of the result, padded with leading zeros
bin2oct.description: Converts a binary number to octal (base 8). Useful in computing contexts where octal notation is used, such as Unix file permissions.

args: Probability of success on each trial
TRUE for the cumulative probability up to number_s; FALSE for the exact probability
binom.dist.description: Returns the binomial distribution probability for exactly k successes in n independent trials. Use it for quality control, survey analysis, and any yes/no experiment - for instance, the probability of exactly 3 heads in 10 coin flips.

args: Probability of success on each trial
Number of successes (lower bound of the range)
Upper bound of the range; if omitted, returns the probability of exactly number_s successes
binom.dist.range.description: Returns the probability that the number of successes in a binomial trial falls within a specified range. Use it when you want "between k₁ and k₂ successes" rather than an exact count.
examples: =BINOM.DIST.RANGE(10, 0.5, 3, 7) returns P(3 ≤ X ≤ 7) for 10 fair coin flips.

args: Probability of success on each trial
Criterion value: the cumulative probability threshold, between 0 and 1
binom.inv.description: Returns the smallest number of successes such that the cumulative binomial probability reaches at least a given value. Use it to find the threshold count for a given confidence level in quality or acceptance sampling.

args: Probability of success on each trial
TRUE for cumulative distribution; FALSE for exact probability
binomdist.description: Compatibility name for BINOM.DIST. Kept for compatibility with Excel 2007 and earlier workbooks. Prefer BINOM.DIST in new formulas.

bitand.description: Returns a bitwise AND of two numbers - the result has a 1 bit only where both inputs have a 1 bit. Use it for flag testing, permission masking, or any low-level binary calculation.

args: Number of bit positions to shift left
bitlshift.description: Shifts a number's binary representation left by a given number of bits, multiplying by 2 for each shift. Use it for bit manipulation in data encoding or protocol analysis.

bitor.description: Returns a bitwise OR of two numbers - the result has a 1 bit wherever either input has a 1. Use it to combine bit flags or to set specific bits in a value.

args: Number of bit positions to shift right
bitrshift.description: Shifts a number's bits right by a given count, dividing by 2 for each shift. Use it to extract the upper bits of a value or to reverse a left shift.

bitxor.description: Returns a bitwise XOR (exclusive OR) of two numbers - the result has a 1 bit where the inputs differ. Use it for checksum calculations or to toggle specific bits.

args: Array or range to process, one column at a time
LAMBDA that receives a single column and returns a scalar
bycol.description: Applies a LAMBDA or built-in function to each column of an array and returns one result per column. Use it to compute column-wise summaries - like summing or averaging each column of a data block - without writing separate formulas for each column.
examples: =BYCOL(A1:D10, LAMBDA(col, SUM(col))) returns the sum of each of the four columns as a single row.

args: Array or range to process, one row at a time
LAMBDA that receives a single row and returns a scalar
byrow.description: Applies a LAMBDA or built-in function to each row of an array and returns one result per row. Use it to compute row-wise summaries - for instance, the maximum value in each row of a multi-column dataset.
examples: =BYROW(A1:D10, LAMBDA(row, MAX(row))) returns the row maximum for each of the 10 rows.

ceiling.description: Rounds a number up to the nearest multiple of a significance value. Use it when you need to round up to a specific increment - for example, always rounding prices up to the nearest $0.05 or time up to the next 15 minutes.

args: 0 rounds negative numbers away from zero; 1 rounds them toward zero
ceiling.math.description: Rounds a number up to the nearest multiple of significance, with a mode argument that controls how negative numbers are handled. Use it when you need consistent rounding for both positive and negative values.

args: Multiple to round up to; always rounds away from zero
ceiling.precise.description: Rounds a number up toward positive infinity to the nearest multiple of significance, regardless of sign. Use it when you always want to round toward positive infinity for both positive and negative numbers.

args: Category of information to return, e.g. "address", "format", "type", "width"
Cell to inspect; defaults to the last changed cell if omitted
cell.description: Returns information about a cell's formatting, location, or contents - such as its address, column width, or format code. Use it for dynamic reporting where you need to inspect cell metadata rather than its value.

args: Code point between 1 and 255 in the system character set
char.description: Returns the character corresponding to a given character code (ASCII or Unicode). Use it to insert special characters - a line break (CHAR(10)), a tab (CHAR(9)), or any character that can't be typed directly - into text formulas.

chidist.description: Compatibility name for CHISQ.DIST.RT (right-tail chi-squared distribution). Prefer CHISQ.DIST.RT in new formulas.

chiinv.description: Compatibility name for CHISQ.INV.RT. Prefer CHISQ.INV.RT in new formulas.

args: The value at which to evaluate the distribution
TRUE for the cumulative distribution function; FALSE for the probability density function
chisq.dist.description: Returns the chi-squared distribution probability (left-tailed CDF or PDF). Use it in goodness-of-fit tests and tests of independence when analyzing categorical data.
examples: =CHISQ.DIST(5, 3, TRUE) returns the left-tail CDF at 5 with 3 degrees of freedom.

chisq.dist.rt.description: Returns the right-tailed chi-squared distribution probability. Use it to find the p-value for a chi-squared test statistic - values close to 0 indicate statistical significance.

chisq.inv.description: Returns the inverse of the left-tailed chi-squared distribution - the value at which the CDF equals a given probability. Use it to find percentile values or critical values for chi-squared tests.
examples: =CHISQ.INV(0.95, 3) returns the 95th percentile with 3 degrees of freedom.

chisq.inv.rt.description: Returns the inverse of the right-tailed chi-squared distribution. Use it to find the critical value at a given significance level for a chi-squared test.
examples: =CHISQ.INV.RT(0.05, 3) returns the critical value at the 5% level.

chisq.test.description: Returns the p-value for a chi-squared test of independence between observed and expected frequencies. Use it to determine whether an association between two categorical variables is statistically significant.

chitest.description: Compatibility name for CHISQ.TEST. Prefer CHISQ.TEST in new formulas.

args: 1-based position of the value to return
choose.description: Returns a value from a list based on an index number. Use it to map a numeric code to a corresponding label or value - like converting 1, 2, 3 into "Low", "Medium", "High" without nested IFs.

args: Index of the first column to include; negative counts from the end
choosecols.description: Returns the specified columns from an array, in the order you list them. Use it to reorder or select a subset of columns from a table without writing multiple INDEX formulas.
examples: =CHOOSECOLS(A1:D10, 3, 1) returns columns C and A of the table (in that order).

args: Index of the first row to include; negative counts from the end
chooserows.description: Returns the specified rows from an array. Use it to extract or reorder rows by position - for instance, to pull specific rows from a result set.

args: String to strip of non-printable characters
clean.description: Removes non-printable characters (the first 32 ASCII control characters) from a text string. Use it to scrub imported data that contains hidden control characters causing display or formula problems.
examples: =CLEAN(A2) strips any control characters from the text.

args: String whose first character's code point you want
code.description: Returns the numeric character code of the first character in a text string. The inverse of CHAR - use it to find the code for an unusual character so you can reference it with CHAR or identify unexpected characters in data.

args: Cell or range whose column number to return; uses the formula cell if omitted
column.description: Returns the column number of a reference (or the current cell if no reference is given). Use it to generate column-position helpers, to build dynamic ranges, or in array formulas that need to know the column index.

args: Range or array whose column count you want
columns.description: Returns the number of columns in a range or array. Use it when you need to know the width of a dynamic range - for instance, to construct an offset or to validate array dimensions.

args: Number of items to choose, without regard to order
combin.description: Returns the number of combinations of n items taken k at a time, ignoring order. Use it in probability and statistics to count how many ways you can choose a subset from a larger group.
examples: =COMBIN(10, 3) returns 120 (the number of ways to choose 3 from 10).

args: Number of items to choose, allowing repetition
combina.description: Returns the number of combinations with repetition allowed (multiset coefficients). Use it when items can be chosen more than once - for instance, counting selections from a menu where items repeat.

args: Real part of the complex number
Imaginary part of the complex number
"i" or "j" as the imaginary unit suffix; defaults to "i"
complex.description: Creates a complex number text string from real and imaginary parts in the form "a+bi". Use it as the starting point for complex-number calculations in engineering or signal analysis.

args: First value or range to join
concat.description: Joins multiple text strings or ranges together into one string, with no separator between them. Use it to combine text from several cells - for instance, assembling a full name from first-name and last-name columns.
examples: =CONCAT(A2, " ", B2) joins first and last names with a space.

concatenate.description: Joins text strings together - an older function superseded by CONCAT. CONCAT is preferred because it also accepts ranges, while CONCATENATE requires individual cell references listed one by one.

confidence.description: Compatibility name for CONFIDENCE.NORM. Prefer CONFIDENCE.NORM in new formulas.

confidence.norm.description: Returns the margin of error for a population mean using the normal distribution. Use it to build a confidence interval: the true mean is likely between AVERAGE ± CONFIDENCE.NORM.
examples: =CONFIDENCE.NORM(0.05, STDEV(A2:A100), COUNT(A2:A100)) returns the margin for a 95% confidence interval.

confidence.t.description: Like CONFIDENCE.NORM but uses the Student's t-distribution, appropriate for small samples where the population standard deviation is unknown. Use it when your sample size is small (typically under 30).
examples: =CONFIDENCE.T(0.05, STDEV(A2:A20), 20) returns the margin for 20 data points.

convert.description: Converts a measurement from one unit to another - miles to kilometers, pounds to kilograms, inches to centimeters, and many more. Use it whenever you need to bridge different unit systems without hand-coding conversion factors.
examples: =CONVERT(100, "mi", "km") returns the equivalent distance in kilometers.

correl.description: Returns the Pearson correlation coefficient (r) between two datasets, ranging from −1 to 1. Use it to measure how strongly two variables move together - for instance, whether advertising spend correlates with sales.

cos.description: Returns the cosine of an angle given in radians. Use it in geometry, physics, and engineering wherever the cosine function is needed.

cosh.description: Returns the hyperbolic cosine of a number. Used in engineering and physics - for instance, in catenary curve calculations and heat transfer problems.

args: Angle in radians; cannot be a multiple of π
cot.description: Returns the cotangent of an angle in radians (cosine divided by sine). A derived trigonometric function used in some geometry and physics applications.

coth.description: Returns the hyperbolic cotangent of a number. Appears in advanced mathematical and physical analysis.

args: First value or range to count numeric entries in
count.description: Counts the number of cells that contain numeric values in a range. Use it to know how many valid data points you have before computing averages or to verify data completeness.
examples: =COUNT(A2:A100) returns how many cells in the range hold numbers.

args: First value or range to count non-empty entries in
counta.description: Counts all non-empty cells in a range regardless of content type. Use it when your data includes text, numbers, and logical values and you want the total number of entries, not just numeric ones.

args: Range whose empty cells you want to count
countblank.description: Counts the empty cells in a range. Use it to find missing data - a high COUNTBLANK on a critical column signals incomplete records that need attention.
examples: =COUNTBLANK(A2:A100) returns how many cells have no content.

args: Cells to evaluate against the condition
countif.description: Counts cells in a range that meet a single condition. Use it to tally how many records match a criterion - how many sales are in the West region, how many scores exceed 90, etc.

countifs.description: Counts cells that meet multiple conditions simultaneously. Use it when you need to count records matching two or more filters at once.
examples: =COUNTIFS(B2:B100, "West", C2:C100, ">1000") counts rows where region is West and sales exceed 1000.

args: Second dataset, same size as array1
covar.description: Compatibility name for COVARIANCE.P (population covariance). Prefer COVARIANCE.P in new formulas.

args: Second dataset, same size as array1
covariance.p.description: Returns the population covariance of two datasets - a measure of how much they vary together. Use it in finance and statistics to quantify the relationship between two variables when you have the entire population.

args: Second dataset, same size as array1
covariance.s.description: Returns the sample covariance, dividing by n−1 instead of n - appropriate when your data is a sample rather than the full population. Use it alongside CORREL or as a building block for more advanced statistical analysis.

args: Probability of success on each trial
critbinom.description: Compatibility name for BINOM.INV. Returns the smallest value for which the cumulative binomial distribution is greater than or equal to alpha. Prefer BINOM.INV in new formulas.

args: Angle in radians; cannot be a multiple of π
csc.description: Returns the cosecant of an angle in radians (1 divided by sine). Used in trigonometry where the reciprocal of sine is needed.

csch.description: Returns the hyperbolic cosecant of a number. Appears in advanced mathematics and physics.

args: First period to include in the sum (1-based)
Last period to include in the sum
cumipmt.description: Returns the cumulative interest paid on a loan between two payment periods. Use it to find out how much total interest you paid during a specific part of a loan - for instance, during year two of a five-year mortgage.
examples: =CUMIPMT(rate, nper, pv, start_period, end_period, type) returns the total interest over that span.

cumprinc.description: Returns the cumulative principal paid on a loan between two periods. Use it alongside CUMIPMT to understand what portion of your payments reduced the balance during a given window.
examples: =CUMPRINC(rate, nper, pv, 13, 24, 0) returns principal paid in months 13 through 24.

args: Month number (1-12); values outside this range roll over to adjacent months
Day number; values outside the valid range roll over similarly
date.description: Creates a proper date value from separate year, month, and day numbers. Use it when you need to assemble a date from components stored in different cells - for instance, building a due date from a year column, a month column, and a day column.

args: Interval to measure: "Y" years, "M" months, "D" days, "MD" days ignoring months, "YM" months ignoring years, "YD" days ignoring years
datedif.description: Calculates the difference between two dates in the unit you specify - years, months, or days. Use it most often to calculate someone's age in complete years, or the number of complete months between two events.
examples: =DATEDIF(B2, TODAY(), "Y") returns a person's age in whole years.

args: Date expressed as a recognisable text string, e.g. "Jan 15, 2025"
datevalue.description: Converts a date stored as text (such as "January 15, 2025") into a numeric date serial that Excel can sort and calculate with. Use it when importing data where dates arrived as text strings.
examples: =DATEVALUE("2025-01-15") returns the serial number for that date.

args: Full range including its header row
Column header name or 1-based column index
daverage.description: Average of values in a database (range with row headers) that match the criteria.
examples: =DAVERAGE(A1:D100, "Sales", F1:F2) Average of values in the column 'Sales' satisfying the criteria in F1:F2.

args: Date value from which to extract the day number (1-31)
day.description: Extracts the day of the month (1–31) from a date value. Use it to isolate the day component - for example, to check whether a transaction occurred in the first or second half of the month.

days.description: Returns the number of days between two dates. Use it for simple deadline calculations or duration tracking - it's just a more readable way to subtract two dates.
examples: =DAYS(B2, A2) returns how many days elapsed between the date in A2 and the date in B2.

args: FALSE or omitted for the US convention; TRUE for the European convention
days360.description: Calculates the number of days between two dates using a 360-day year (twelve 30-day months). Use it for financial calculations - such as interest accrual - that follow a 30/360 day-count convention.
examples: =DAYS360(A2, B2) returns the day count under the 30/360 assumption.

args: Residual value at the end of its life
Number of periods in the asset's useful life
Months in the first year; defaults to 12
db.description: Returns the depreciation of an asset for a given period using the fixed-declining-balance method. Use it when accounting rules require declining-balance depreciation - common for machinery, vehicles, and equipment.
examples: =DB(cost, salvage, life, period) returns the depreciation charge for that period.

args: Full range including its header row
Column to count, by header name or 1-based index
dcount.description: Counts the cells containing numbers in a database that match the criteria.
examples: =DCOUNT(A1:D50, "Salary", G1:G2) counts rows with numeric salaries matching your criteria.

args: Full range including its header row
Column to count non-empty entries in
dcounta.description: Counts all non-empty cells in a database column that match the criteria - including text entries. Use it instead of DCOUNT when the column you're counting contains text, such as names or status codes.
examples: =DCOUNTA(A1:D50, "Name", G1:G2) counts all non-blank name entries matching your filter.

args: Residual value at end of useful life
Number of periods in the asset's useful life
Depreciation rate multiplier; defaults to 2 for double-declining balance
ddb.description: Calculates depreciation using the double-declining-balance method (or any other factor you specify). Use it for assets that lose value faster in early years - it produces higher charges at the start and lower amounts later.
examples: =DDB(cost, salvage, life, period, 2) applies double the straight-line rate.

args: Decimal value to convert; must be between -512 and 511
Minimum width of the result, padded with leading zeros
dec2bin.description: Converts a decimal integer to binary. Use it to translate regular numbers into binary format for digital logic work or data encoding.

args: Minimum width of the result, padded with leading zeros
dec2hex.description: Converts a decimal integer to hexadecimal. Use it when working with memory addresses, color codes, or any system that uses hex notation.

args: Decimal value to convert; must be between -536870912 and 536870911
Minimum width of the result, padded with leading zeros
dec2oct.description: Converts a decimal integer to octal (base 8). Useful in Unix/Linux contexts where file permissions and other values use octal notation.

args: String representation of the number in the given base
Base of the input number, between 2 and 36
decimal.description: Converts a text representation of a number in a given base into its decimal equivalent. Use it when you have numbers in binary, octal, or hexadecimal stored as text and need a decimal number to calculate with.

args: Angle in radians to convert to degrees
degrees.description: Converts an angle from radians to degrees. Use it after trigonometric functions that return results in radians when you want the angle expressed in degrees for display or further calculation.

args: Second value to compare against; defaults to 0
delta.description: Tests whether two numbers are equal, returning 1 if they are and 0 otherwise - the Kronecker delta function. Use it as a compact equality check whose result can be used directly in arithmetic.

devsq.description: Returns the sum of squared deviations from the mean for a dataset: Σ(xᵢ−x̄)². Use it as an intermediate step in variance or regression calculations.

args: Full range including its header row
Column header name or 1-based column index
dget.description: Single value from a database column that matches the specified criteria, returning an error if more than one row matches.
examples: =DGET(A1:D50, "Phone", G1:G2) returns the phone number of the one employee matching your criteria.

disc.description: Returns the discount rate for a security — the annualised spread between its purchase price and redemption value.
examples: =DISC(A2, B2, 97.5, 100, 0) returns the discount rate for a T-bill.

args: Full table including its header row
Column to find the maximum in
Criteria range with a header and a condition
dmax.description: Returns the largest value in a database column that meets the given criteria. Use it to find the highest figure in a filtered subset - for example, the maximum sale amount for a specific product category.
examples: =DMAX(A1:D100, "Amount", F1:F2) returns the largest matching sale.

args: Full table including its header row
Column to find the minimum in
Criteria range with a header and a condition
dmin.description: Returns the smallest value in a database column that meets the given criteria. Use it to find the lowest recorded value in a filtered set - the counterpart of DMAX.
examples: =DMIN(A1:D100, "Amount", F1:F2) returns the smallest matching sale.

args: Value to format as a currency text string
Number of decimal places; negative rounds left of the decimal point
dollar.description: Converts a number to text formatted as currency, with a specified number of decimal places and a dollar sign. Use it to embed a formatted currency value inside a text string - for example, in a generated message.

args: Price in the form integer.fraction, e.g. 1.02 means 1 and 2/n
dollarde.description: Converts a price expressed in fractional dollar notation (as used in bond markets, e.g., 1.02 meaning 1 and 2/32) to a decimal price. Use it when your data comes from markets that quote in fractional form.

args: Denominator to use in the fractional representation
dollarfr.description: Converts a decimal price back to fractional dollar notation. The reverse of DOLLARDE - use it when you need to display a price in the market's native fractional format.

args: Full table including its header row
Criteria range with a header and a condition
dproduct.description: Multiplies all values in a database column that match the criteria and returns the result. Use it when you need a running product over a filtered subset of numbers - a rare but occasionally necessary calculation in scientific or financial models.
examples: =DPRODUCT(A1:D20, "Factor", G1:G2) multiplies all factor values that match the conditions.

args: Rows to drop from the top; negative drops from the bottom
Columns to drop from the left; negative drops from the right
drop.description: Removes a specified number of rows or columns from the start or end of an array. Use it to skip header rows or to trim trailing rows from a returned range before using it in further calculations.
examples: =DROP(A1:D10, 1) removes the first row; =DROP(A1:D10, -2) removes the last two rows.

args: Full table including its header row
Column to measure, by header name or 1-based index
Criteria range with a header and a condition
dstdev.description: Estimates the standard deviation of values in a database column matching the criteria, treating the matching rows as a sample. Use it to measure the spread of a filtered subset of data.
examples: =DSTDEV(A1:D100, "Score", F1:F2) estimates the variability of scores in the matched group.

args: Full table including its header row
Column to measure, treating matches as the whole population
Criteria range with a header and a condition
dstdevp.description: Like DSTDEV, but treats the filtered rows as the entire population rather than a sample. Use it when the matching records represent the complete group, not just a portion of it.

args: Full table including its header row
Column to sum, by header name or 1-based index
Criteria range with a header and a condition
dsum.description: Adds up all values in a database column that match the specified criteria. A classic use case is totaling sales for a particular product or region within a larger table.
examples: =DSUM(A1:D100, "Revenue", F1:F2) sums all revenue rows where the criteria are met.

args: Full table including its header row
Column to measure, treating matches as a sample
Criteria range with a header and a condition
dvar.description: Estimates the variance of values in a database column matching the criteria, treating the result as a sample. Variance measures how spread out values are; DVAR gives you that for a filtered subset.

args: Full table including its header row
Column to measure, treating matches as the whole population
Criteria range with a header and a condition
dvarp.description: Like DVAR, but assumes the matched records represent the whole population rather than a sample. Use it when your filter captures every relevant record.

args: Months to offset; positive moves forward, negative moves back
edate.description: Returns the date that is a specified number of months before or after a start date, landing on the same day of the month. Use it to calculate subscription renewals, installment due dates, or contract end dates.
examples: =EDATE(A2, 6) returns the date six months after the date in A2.

args: Annual nominal interest rate as a decimal
Number of compounding periods per year
effect.description: Returns the effective annual interest rate given a nominal rate and the number of compounding periods per year. Use it to compare loan or investment products that compound at different frequencies - daily compounding at 5% is not the same as annual 5%.
examples: =EFFECT(0.05, 12) returns the effective rate with monthly compounding.

args: Months to offset; 0 returns the last day of start_date's own month
eomonth.description: Returns the last day of the month a given number of months before or after a start date. Use it to generate month-end reporting dates or to find when a monthly period closes.
examples: =EOMONTH(A2, 0) returns the last day of the month containing A2; =EOMONTH(A2, 1) returns the last day of the following month.

erf.description: Returns the error function integrated between 0 and a given value. Used in statistics and physics for normal distribution calculations and diffusion problems.

erf.precise.description: A single-argument version of ERF that always integrates from 0 to the given upper limit, with higher numerical precision. Use it when you need the standard error function with more accuracy.

erfc.description: Returns the complementary error function: 1 minus ERF. Use it when you need the probability that a value lies beyond a threshold in a normal distribution context.

erfc.precise.description: A higher-precision version of ERFC. Use it in scientific and engineering applications where numerical accuracy is critical.

errortype.description: Returns a number corresponding to the type of error in a cell (1 for #NULL!, 2 for #DIV/0!, etc.). Use it inside error-handling formulas to take different actions depending on which error occurred.

args: Value to round up to the nearest even integer
even.description: Rounds a number up to the nearest even integer. Use it when you need to ensure a quantity is even - for instance, packaging items that must come in pairs.

exact.description: Compares two text strings and returns TRUE only if they are character-for-character identical, including case. Use it when case-sensitivity matters - "Apple" and "apple" are not equal under EXACT even though = treats them as the same.

args: Exponent to raise the mathematical constant e to
exp.description: Returns e (Euler's number ≈ 2.71828) raised to the power of a given number. Use it for exponential growth and decay models, as the inverse of LN, or in any formula involving the mathematical constant e.

args: Value to fill the added cells with; defaults to #N/A
expand.description: Expands an array to a specified number of rows and columns, filling new cells with a pad value (default #N/A). Use it to ensure an array has a consistent size before combining it with other arrays of fixed dimensions.
examples: =EXPAND(A1:B3, 5, 3, 0) extends the range to 5 rows and 3 columns, padding with 0.

args: TRUE for the cumulative distribution; FALSE for the probability density
expon.dist.description: Returns the exponential distribution probability - either the cumulative or probability density. Use it to model the time between events in a Poisson process, such as the probability that a customer arrives within the next minute.

args: TRUE for cumulative distribution; FALSE for probability density
expondist.description: Compatibility name for EXPON.DIST. Prefer EXPON.DIST in new formulas.

f.dist.description: Returns the F-distribution probability (left-tailed CDF or PDF). Use it in ANOVA and regression analysis when evaluating variance ratios.
examples: =F.DIST(2.5, 5, 10, TRUE) returns the left-tail probability for an F-statistic of 2.5.

f.dist.rt.description: Returns the right-tailed F-distribution probability. Use it to find the p-value for an F-test - values near 0 indicate that the variances are significantly different.

f.inv.description: Returns the inverse of the left-tailed F-distribution - the F-value at which the CDF equals a given probability. Use it to find critical values for F-tests or to construct F-distribution confidence intervals.

f.inv.rt.description: Returns the inverse of the right-tailed F-distribution. Use it to find the critical F-value at a given significance level for an F-test.
examples: =F.INV.RT(0.05, 5, 10) returns the critical value at the 5% level.

f.test.description: Returns the p-value for an F-test comparing the variances of two datasets. Use it to determine whether two groups have significantly different variability before applying certain other statistical tests.

args: Non-negative integer whose factorial you want
fact.description: Returns the factorial of a non-negative integer (n! = 1 × 2 × … × n). Use it in combinatorics to count the number of ways to arrange items, or in probability formulas that require factorials.

args: Value for which to compute the double factorial (n!!)
factdouble.description: Returns the double factorial of a number (n!! = n × (n−2) × (n−4) × … down to 1 or 2). Used in combinatorics and probability, particularly with Gaussian integrals and binomial series.

false.description: Returns the logical value FALSE. Use it in formulas that require a literal FALSE as a function argument - for instance, as the match_type parameter in VLOOKUP to force an exact match.
examples: =VLOOKUP(A2, B:C, 2, FALSE) performs an exact lookup.

fdist.description: Compatibility name for F.DIST.RT (right-tail F-distribution). Prefer F.DIST.RT in new formulas.

args: Boolean array of the same height (or width) indicating which rows (or columns) to keep
Value to return when every row is filtered out; omitting causes a #CALC! error
filter.description: Returns only the rows (or columns) from a range that meet a specified condition. Use it instead of AutoFilter when you want filtered results to spill directly into formulas - the output updates automatically as data changes.
examples: =FILTER(A2:D100, C2:C100="West") returns all rows where column C is "West".

args: 1-based character position to start the search from
find.description: Returns the position of one text string within another, case-sensitively. Use it to locate exactly where a substring begins - then combine with MID or LEFT to extract the relevant portion.
examples: =FIND("@", A2) returns the position of the "@" in an email address.

findb.description: Like FIND but counts positions in bytes rather than characters - relevant for double-byte East Asian character sets. Use it when working with mixed single- and double-byte text in CJK locales.
examples: =FINDB("A", A2) returns the byte position of "A".

finv.description: Compatibility name for F.INV.RT. Prefer F.INV.RT in new formulas.

fisher.description: Returns the Fisher transformation of a correlation coefficient r, converting it to a value with approximately normal distribution. Use it to construct confidence intervals for r or to test whether two correlations are significantly different.

fisherinv.description: Returns the inverse of the Fisher transformation - converts a Fisher z-value back to a correlation coefficient. Use it after computing confidence intervals in z-space to report results as r-values.

args: Decimal places to show; defaults to 2
TRUE to suppress the thousands separator
fixed.description: Rounds a number to a specified number of decimal places and returns it as formatted text, optionally with thousands separators. Use it to embed a formatted number inside a text formula without changing the cell's number format.

floor.description: Rounds a number down to the nearest multiple of a significance value. Use it when you always want to round down to a specific increment - for example, discarding partial hours when computing billable time.

args: 0 rounds negative numbers toward negative infinity; 1 rounds them toward zero
floor.math.description: Rounds a number down to the nearest multiple of significance, with a mode argument controlling behavior for negative numbers. More flexible than FLOOR when you need consistent rounding across positive and negative values.

args: Multiple to round down to; always rounds toward negative infinity
floor.precise.description: Rounds a number down toward negative infinity to the nearest multiple of significance, regardless of sign. Use it for consistent rounding direction across both positive and negative numbers.

args: The data point whose value you want to predict
forecast.description: Predicts a future value along a linear trend fitted to existing data. Equivalent to FORECAST.LINEAR.
examples: =FORECAST(13, B2:B10, A2:A10) predicts the y-value at x=13.

args: The data point whose value you want to predict
forecast.linear.description: Predicts a future value by fitting a straight line through existing x/y pairs using least squares regression.
examples: =FORECAST.LINEAR(13, B2:B10, A2:A10) predicts the y-value at x=13.

args: Cell containing the formula you want to see as text
formulatext.description: Returns the formula in a referenced cell as a text string. Use it to display or audit formulas - for example, to show the formula alongside its result in a report.
examples: =FORMULATEXT(B2) returns the text of whatever formula is in B2.

args: Upper boundary of each bin; must be sorted ascending
frequency.description: Returns a vertical array of counts showing how many values fall into each of the specified bins. Returns one more element than bins_array to catch values above the highest bin.
examples: =FREQUENCY(A2:A20, C2:C5) counts scores into bands defined by C2:C5.

ftest.description: Compatibility name for F.TEST. Prefer F.TEST in new formulas.

args: Present value (lump sum at the start); defaults to 0
fv.description: Calculates the future value of an investment based on regular payments and a constant interest rate. Use it to project how much money you'll have after making regular contributions - like a retirement savings plan.
examples: =FV(rate, nper, pmt) returns the total amount accumulated.

args: Array of interest rates to apply in successive periods
fvschedule.description: Returns the future value of an initial principal after applying a schedule of variable interest rates — useful for modelling adjustable-rate loans or investments.
examples: =FVSCHEDULE(1000, {0.05, 0.04, 0.06}) compounds 1000 over three years at different rates.

args: Positive real number (non-integer negatives are also accepted)
gamma.description: Returns the value of the gamma function Γ(x), which extends the factorial to non-integer values (Γ(n) = (n-1)! for positive integers). Use it in advanced probability and statistics.

args: Shape parameter of the gamma distribution
Scale parameter of the gamma distribution
gamma.inv.description: Returns the inverse of the gamma cumulative distribution - the value at which the CDF equals a given probability. Use it for quantile calculations with gamma-distributed data.

args: TRUE for the cumulative distribution; FALSE for the probability density
gammadist.description: Returns the gamma distribution probability - an older function name for what is now GAMMA.DIST. Use it to model waiting times or other positive-valued data following a gamma distribution.

gammainv.description: Compatibility name for GAMMA.INV. Prefer GAMMA.INV in new formulas.

gammaln.description: Returns the natural logarithm of the gamma function. Use it to avoid numeric overflow when computing large factorials or gamma values - working in log-space keeps numbers manageable.

gammaln.precise.description: A higher-precision version of GAMMALN, using a more accurate algorithm. Use it when numerical precision matters in scientific or statistical computations.

gauss.description: Returns the probability that a standard normal variable falls between 0 and z - equal to Φ(z) − 0.5. Use it when you need the one-sided probability from the mean to a specific z-score.

gcd.description: Returns the greatest common divisor of two or more integers - the largest number that divides all of them evenly. Use it to simplify fractions or to find the largest common factor shared between quantities.

geomean.description: Returns the geometric mean of a set of positive numbers. Use it to average rates of change or growth rates - for instance, the average annual return of an investment, where the geometric mean correctly accounts for compounding.

gestep.description: Returns 1 if a number is greater than or equal to a step value, and 0 otherwise. Use it to implement threshold comparisons in engineering models without writing an IF formula.

args: New x values for which to predict y
TRUE (default) fits the constant b; FALSE forces b=1
growth.description: Returns predicted y-values along an exponential trend curve fitted to known data. The exponential counterpart to TREND.
examples: =GROWTH(B2:B6, A2:A6, A7:A10) predicts future values from an exponential curve.

harmean.description: Returns the harmonic mean of a set of numbers. Use it to average rates - speeds, prices per unit, or yields - where the reciprocal relationship matters more than simple averaging.

args: Hexadecimal string to convert (up to 10 characters)
Minimum width of the result, padded with leading zeros
hex2bin.description: Converts a hexadecimal number to binary. Use it to inspect the individual bits of a hex value - common in network protocols and hardware analysis.

hex2dec.description: Converts a hexadecimal number to decimal. Use it to translate memory addresses, web color codes, or protocol fields into regular numbers.

args: Minimum width of the result, padded with leading zeros
hex2oct.description: Converts a hexadecimal number to octal. A utility for moving between hex and octal representations when both notations appear in the same system.

args: Value to search for in the first row of the table
Table to search, with the key row at the top
1-based row number within the table to return a value from
TRUE for approximate match (first row must be sorted); FALSE for exact match
hlookup.description: Looks up a value in the first row of a table and returns a value from the same column a specified number of rows down. Use it for horizontal lookup tables where categories are in the first row and data sits below them.
examples: =HLOOKUP("Q2", A1:D5, 3, FALSE) finds "Q2" in row 1 and returns the value in row 3 of that column.

args: Time or date-time value from which to extract the hour (0-23)
hour.description: Extracts the hour component (0–23) from a time value. Use it to group transactions by time of day or to filter records to a specific hour.

args: Additional arrays to append to the right
hstack.description: Combines multiple arrays or ranges side by side (horizontally) into a single array. Use it to merge separate datasets that have the same number of rows into one wider table.
examples: =HSTACK(A1:B10, D1:E10) joins two two-column ranges into a single four-column array.

hypgeom.dist.description: Returns the hypergeometric distribution probability - the probability of exactly k successes when drawing n items without replacement from a population of N containing K successes. Use it for quality sampling when items are not returned after each draw.

hypgeomdist.description: Compatibility name for HYPGEOM.DIST (non-cumulative form). Prefer HYPGEOM.DIST in new formulas.

args: Value to return when the condition is TRUE
Value to return when the condition is FALSE; returns FALSE if omitted
if.description: Returns one value when a condition is TRUE and a different value when it is FALSE. The most fundamental branching function in Excel - use it whenever your output depends on whether a condition is met.
examples: =IF(A2>0, "Positive", "Zero or negative") labels each row based on its value.

args: Value to return if the expression produces any error
iferror.description: Returns a value you specify if a formula produces any error; otherwise returns the formula's normal result. Use it to replace error codes with a blank, zero, or friendly message - most often around VLOOKUP or division formulas.

args: Value to return specifically when the expression returns #N/A
ifna.description: Returns the value you specify only if the formula produces #N/A; all other errors pass through unchanged. Use it when you specifically want to catch "not found" from lookups without suppressing other error types.

args: Value to return when test1 is TRUE
Additional test/value pairs evaluated in order
ifs.description: Tests multiple conditions in order and returns the value associated with the first TRUE one. Use it as a cleaner replacement for nested IF formulas when you have three or more distinct branches.
examples: =IFS(A2>=90, "A", A2>=80, "B", A2>=70, "C", TRUE, "F") assigns a letter grade.

args: Complex number as a text string, e.g. "3+4i"
imabs.description: Returns the modulus (absolute value) of a complex number - its distance from the origin in the complex plane. Use it to find the magnitude of a complex quantity such as an impedance or a phasor.

args: Complex number as a text string; returns the imaginary coefficient
imaginary.description: Extracts the imaginary part from a complex number string. Use it when you need just the "b" coefficient from "a+bi" for further calculations.

args: Complex number as a text string; returns the angle in radians
imargument.description: Returns the angle (argument) of a complex number in radians - the angle it makes with the positive real axis. Use it in signal processing to find the phase of a complex quantity.

args: Complex number as a text string
imconjugate.description: Returns the complex conjugate of a complex number - same real part, negated imaginary part. Use it in complex division or AC circuit analysis to simplify expressions.

args: Complex number as a text string
imcos.description: Returns the cosine of a complex number. Used in advanced mathematical and engineering analysis where trigonometric functions take complex arguments.

args: Complex number as a text string
imcosh.description: Returns the hyperbolic cosine of a complex number. Appears in advanced mathematics and physics involving complex arguments to hyperbolic functions.

args: Complex number as a text string
imcot.description: Returns the cotangent of a complex number. A specialized function for advanced mathematical derivations.

args: Complex number as a text string
imcsc.description: Returns the cosecant (1/sin) of a complex number. Used in specialized mathematical computations.

args: Complex number as a text string
imcsch.description: Returns the hyperbolic cosecant of a complex number. Appears in advanced physics and mathematics.

imdiv.description: Divides one complex number by another and returns the result as a complex number. Use it for complex arithmetic in circuit analysis or signal processing.

args: Complex number as a text string
imexp.description: Returns e raised to the power of a complex number. Essential in signal analysis and Fourier-related calculations where complex exponentials appear.

args: Complex number as a text string
imln.description: Returns the natural logarithm of a complex number. Use it in engineering and physics where the log of a complex quantity is needed.

args: Complex number as a text string
imlog10.description: Returns the base-10 logarithm of a complex number. Use it in decibel calculations or other logarithmic engineering measures with complex inputs.

args: Complex number as a text string
imlog2.description: Returns the base-2 logarithm of a complex number. Useful in information theory and digital signal processing with complex inputs.

args: Base complex number as a text string
Exponent to raise the complex number to
impower.description: Raises a complex number to a given power and returns the result as a complex number. Use it in physics, control theory, or mathematical analysis.

args: First complex number as a text string
Additional complex numbers to multiply; at least one more required
improduct.description: Multiplies two or more complex numbers together. Use it to chain complex multiplications in AC circuit or signal-processing calculations.

args: Complex number as a text string; returns the real coefficient
imreal.description: Extracts the real part from a complex number string. Use it when you need just the "a" coefficient from "a+bi" for further arithmetic.

args: Complex number as a text string
imsec.description: Returns the secant (1/cos) of a complex number. A specialized function for advanced mathematical use cases.

args: Complex number as a text string
imsech.description: Returns the hyperbolic secant of a complex number. Appears in advanced mathematical and physical derivations.

args: Complex number as a text string
imsin.description: Returns the sine of a complex number. Used in Fourier analysis, quantum mechanics, and other fields involving complex arguments to sine.

args: Complex number as a text string
imsinh.description: Returns the hyperbolic sine of a complex number. Used in advanced physics and mathematics involving hyperbolic functions of complex arguments.

args: Complex number as a text string
imsqrt.description: Returns the principal square root of a complex number. Use it to take the square root of a negative or complex quantity without getting an error.

imsub.description: Subtracts one complex number from another and returns the result as a complex number. Use it for complex arithmetic - for instance, finding the difference between two phasors.

args: First complex number as a text string
Additional complex numbers to add; at least one more required
imsum.description: Adds two or more complex numbers together. Use it to sum phasors or complex vectors in electrical or mechanical engineering analysis.

args: Complex number as a text string
imtan.description: Returns the tangent of a complex number. A specialized function for advanced mathematical computations.

args: Array or range to retrieve from
1-based row to return; 0 returns the entire column
1-based column to return; 0 returns the entire row
Which area to use when the reference contains multiple separate areas
index.description: Returns the value at a specified row and column within a range. Most often combined with MATCH to create a flexible two-way lookup that works from left to right - unlike VLOOKUP.
examples: =INDEX(B2:D10, MATCH("Alice", A2:A10, 0), 2) returns Alice's value from the second column of the data.

args: Cell address or range as a text string, e.g. "B2" or "Sheet1!A1"
TRUE or omitted for A1-style addresses; FALSE for R1C1-style
indirect.description: Returns a cell or range reference built from a text string, evaluated at runtime. Use it to create dynamic references - for instance, referencing a sheet name stored in a cell, or building a range address from row and column variables.
examples: =INDIRECT("Sheet2!A1") returns whatever is in Sheet2's A1.

args: Category of environment information to return, e.g. "version", "osversion", "numfile"
info.description: Returns information about the current operating environment - such as the Excel version, operating system, or number of sheets. Use it for diagnostics or to make formulas behave differently across environments.

args: Value to round down to the nearest integer (toward negative infinity)
int.description: Rounds a number down to the nearest integer, toward negative infinity. Use it to truncate a decimal to a whole number - but note that for negative numbers, =INT(-4.2) returns -5, not -4; use TRUNC if you always want rounding toward zero.

args: Independent (x) data points, same count as known_ys
intercept.description: Returns the y-intercept of the linear regression line through a set of data points. Use it with SLOPE to build a simple linear model: predicted_y = SLOPE × x + INTERCEPT.
examples: =INTERCEPT(B2:B20, A2:A20) returns the constant term of the best-fit line.

intrate.description: Returns the effective annual interest rate for a fully invested security — one that pays no periodic coupons but instead returns a lump sum at maturity.
examples: =INTRATE(A2, B2, 1000, 1100, 0) returns the annual rate on a zero-coupon security.

args: Period number to calculate the interest portion for (1-based)
Future value after the last payment; defaults to 0
ipmt.description: Returns the interest portion of a specific loan payment. Use it to see exactly how much of a given month's payment goes toward interest versus principal.
examples: =IPMT(rate, period, nper, pv) returns the interest amount for that one period.

args: Cash flow amounts; negative values are outflows, positive are inflows
Initial guess for the rate; defaults to 0.1
irr.description: Calculates the Internal Rate of Return for a series of cash flows. Use it to evaluate whether an investment project is worthwhile - the IRR is the discount rate that makes the NPV equal to zero, and you compare it against your required return.
examples: =IRR(A2:A10) returns the annualized rate of return for the cash-flow series.

args: Expression or cell reference to test for emptiness
isblank.description: Returns TRUE if a cell is completely empty, FALSE otherwise. Use it to check for missing data before performing calculations, or to drive conditional formatting on empty cells.
examples: =ISBLANK(A2) returns TRUE only if A2 has no content at all.

args: Expression to test; returns TRUE for any error except #N/A
iserr.description: Returns TRUE if a cell contains any error except #N/A. Use it when you want to catch all errors except #N/A separately - distinguishing "not found" from other problems.
examples: =ISERR(A2) returns TRUE for #DIV/0!, #VALUE!, and #REF!, but not for #N/A.

args: Expression to test; returns TRUE for any error value
iserror.description: Returns TRUE if a cell contains any error value, including #N/A. Use it as a general error trap to display a friendly message instead of an error code.

args: Number to test for being even
iseven.description: Returns TRUE if a number is even, FALSE if odd. Use it to alternate row styles in a table (combined with ROW()), to flag even quantities, or as a simple parity check.

args: Cell to check for containing a formula
isformula.description: Returns TRUE if a cell contains a formula, FALSE if it contains a plain value. Use it to audit spreadsheets and identify which cells are computed versus hard-coded.
examples: =ISFORMULA(A2) returns TRUE if A2 has a formula.

args: Expression to test for being a logical value (TRUE or FALSE)
islogical.description: Returns TRUE if a cell contains a logical value (TRUE or FALSE). Use it to validate inputs or to distinguish logical values from numbers and text in mixed-type data.

args: Expression to test for the #N/A error specifically
isna.description: Returns TRUE if a cell contains the #N/A error. Use it specifically to detect "not found" results from lookup functions without trapping other error types.

args: Expression to test; returns TRUE for anything that is not a text string
isnontext.description: Returns TRUE if a cell does not contain text - including numbers, logical values, dates, errors, and blanks. Use it to validate that a field contains a non-text value.

args: Expression to test for being a numeric value
isnumber.description: Returns TRUE if a cell contains a number. Use it to validate numeric inputs or to filter mixed-type arrays where only numbers should be processed.
examples: =ISNUMBER(A2) returns TRUE if A2 holds a numeric value.

iso.ceiling.description: Rounds a number up toward positive infinity to the nearest multiple of significance. Equivalent to CEILING.PRECISE - provided for ISO standard compatibility.

args: Number to test for being odd
isodd.description: Returns TRUE if a number is odd, FALSE if even. Use it alongside ISEVEN for alternating logic - for instance, treating odd-numbered periods differently from even ones.

args: A LAMBDA parameter to test for having been omitted by the caller
isomitted.description: Returns TRUE if an argument passed to a LAMBDA is missing (omitted by the caller). Use it inside LAMBDA definitions to detect optional arguments and supply default values.
examples: inside a LAMBDA, =IF(ISOMITTED(x), 0, x) defaults x to 0 when not provided.

args: Date whose ISO week number (1-53, Monday-based) you want
isoweeknum.description: Returns the ISO week number (1–53) of a given date, following the international standard where weeks start on Monday and week 1 contains the year's first Thursday. Use it for scheduling in industries that track work by ISO week numbers.

args: Period number to calculate interest for (0-based)
ispmt.description: Returns the interest paid during a specific period of a loan assuming simple interest (equal principal payments each period). It differs from IPMT in its loan structure assumption. Use it for loans structured on a simple-interest basis.

args: Expression to test for being a cell or range reference
isref.description: Returns TRUE if a value is a valid cell reference. Use it to check whether an INDIRECT formula produced a valid reference before using it.
examples: =ISREF(INDIRECT(A2)) returns TRUE if A2 holds a valid cell address.

args: Expression to test for being a text string
istext.description: Returns TRUE if a cell contains text. Use it to validate text inputs, filter text values in arrays, or guard against text accidentally entering a numeric calculation.

kurt.description: Returns the kurtosis of a dataset - a measure of how heavy the tails are compared to a normal distribution. Positive kurtosis means more extreme outliers than normal; negative means fewer. Use it to understand whether your data has unusual outlier behavior.

args: Name of the first parameter (subsequent arguments add more parameters)
Additional parameter names, with the formula as the final argument
lambda.description: Defines a custom, reusable function with named parameters that you can store in the Name Manager and call like any built-in function. Use it to package a complex formula you repeat often into a named function, making your workbook easier to maintain and understand.
examples: =LAMBDA(x, x^2)(5) returns 25 inline; stored as SQUARE, you can use =SQUARE(5) anywhere.

args: Rank from the top (1 returns the maximum)
large.description: Returns the k-th largest value in a dataset. Use it to find top-N values - the second-highest score, third-largest sale - without sorting the data.

lcm.description: Returns the least common multiple of two or more integers - the smallest number that is a multiple of all the supplied values. Use it to synchronize repeating events or to find a common denominator.

args: Number of characters to extract from the start
left.description: Returns the specified number of characters from the start of a text string. Use it to extract a prefix - for instance, the first three characters of a product code.
examples: =LEFT(A2, 3) returns the first 3 characters of A2.

leftb.description: Like LEFT but counts characters in bytes. Use it in East Asian locales where double-byte characters each take two bytes.

args: String whose character count you want
len.description: Returns the number of characters in a text string. Use it to check entry lengths - validating that a field isn't too long, detecting trailing spaces, or building dynamic formulas that depend on string length.

lenb.description: Returns the number of bytes in a text string, counting double-byte characters as two. Use it in East Asian locales to measure byte length rather than character count.

args: Value or expression to assign to name1
The final argument is the formula that uses the defined names
let.description: Assigns names to intermediate calculation results within a formula, letting you reuse them without repeating the expression. Use it to make complex formulas more readable and to avoid calculating the same subexpression multiple times.
examples: =LET(rate, 0.2, gross, A2*1.15, gross*(1-rate)) computes the net amount.

args: TRUE includes the intercept; FALSE forces it to zero
TRUE returns additional regression statistics; FALSE returns just the coefficients
linest.description: Returns the parameters of a linear regression as an array. Enter as an array formula. When stats is TRUE, five rows of statistics including R² and standard errors are returned.
examples: =LINEST(C2:C10, A2:B10, TRUE, TRUE) returns full regression statistics for two predictors.

args: Positive real number whose natural logarithm you want
ln.description: Returns the natural logarithm of a number (logarithm base e). Use it for exponential growth/decay analysis, probability distributions, and any formula where the natural log is required.

log.description: Returns the logarithm of a number to a specified base (default is 10). Use it when working in a particular base - base-2 for information theory, base-10 for decibels, or a custom base for specialized formulas.

args: Positive real number whose base-10 logarithm you want
log10.description: Returns the base-10 logarithm of a number. Use it for decibel calculations, pH calculations, and any formula where common (base-10) logarithms are the standard.

args: TRUE includes the base constant; FALSE forces it to 1
logest.description: Fits an exponential curve to data and returns the parameters as an array. The exponential equivalent of LINEST.
examples: =LOGEST(B2:B10, A2:A10, TRUE, FALSE) returns the base and multiplier of the best-fit exponential curve.

args: Mean of the underlying normal distribution (ln x)
Standard deviation of the underlying normal distribution
loginv.description: Compatibility name for LOGNORM.INV. Prefer LOGNORM.INV in new formulas.

lognorm.dist.description: Returns the log-normal distribution probability (CDF or PDF). Use it to model data that is the product of many positive factors - stock prices, income distributions, particle sizes.

lognorm.inv.description: Returns the inverse of the log-normal cumulative distribution - the value at a given percentile. Use it to find percentile values for a log-normally distributed variable.

args: Mean of the underlying normal distribution (ln x)
Standard deviation of the underlying normal distribution
lognormdist.description: Compatibility name for LOGNORM.DIST (cumulative form). Prefer LOGNORM.DIST in new formulas.

args: Sorted single-row or single-column range to search in
Range to return a value from; defaults to lookup_vector
lookup.description: Searches a one-row or one-column range for a value and returns a corresponding value from another range. An older function largely superseded by VLOOKUP and XLOOKUP, but still useful for approximate-match lookups in sorted data.
examples: =LOOKUP(B2, rate_column, bracket_column) returns the bracket corresponding to B2.

lower.description: Converts all letters in a text string to lowercase. Use it to normalize text before comparing or storing it - for instance, ensuring all email addresses are consistently lowercase.

args: Number of rows in the output array
Number of columns in the output array
LAMBDA(row, col) called for each cell to produce its value
makearray.description: Creates an array of a specified size by calling a LAMBDA with the 1-based row and column index for each cell.
examples: =MAKEARRAY(3, 4, LAMBDA(r, c, r*c)) builds a 3×4 multiplication table.

args: First array to pass element-wise to the LAMBDA
Second array (additional arrays may follow before the LAMBDA)
LAMBDA that receives one element from each array and returns a value; must be the last argument
map.description: Applies a LAMBDA function to each element of one or more arrays and returns an array of results. Use it to transform every value in a list with a custom calculation - a type-safe alternative to array formulas.
examples: =MAP(A2:A10, LAMBDA(x, x^2)) returns the squares of each value.

args: Single-row or single-column range to search
-1 finds the smallest value >= lookup_value (descending order required); 0 finds the first exact match; 1 finds the largest value <= lookup_value (ascending order required)
match.description: Searches for a value in a range and returns its relative position. Most often paired with INDEX for flexible lookups; also useful on its own to find where an item sits in a list.
examples: =MATCH("Alice", A2:A100, 0) returns the row number of "Alice" in the range.

max.description: Returns the largest value in a set of numbers. Use it to find the peak figure in a list - highest sales amount, latest date, or fastest time.
examples: =MAX(A2:A100) returns the largest value in the range.

args: First value or range; text counts as 0, TRUE as 1, FALSE as 0
maxa.description: Returns the largest value in a range, treating text as 0 and TRUE as 1. Use it when you want the maximum across a mixed-type range and need non-numeric values to be counted rather than skipped.
examples: =MAXA(A2:A20) returns the maximum even if the range contains text.

args: Cells to find the maximum in
Additional criteria for the extra ranges
maxifs.description: Returns the maximum value among cells that meet one or more conditions. Use it to find the highest value within a filtered group - for instance, the largest sale in the West region.
examples: =MAXIFS(C2:C100, B2:B100, "West") returns the largest value in C where B is "West".

args: Square numeric matrix (same number of rows and columns)
mdeterm.description: Returns the determinant of a square matrix. Use it in linear algebra - for instance, to check whether a system of equations has a unique solution (a non-zero determinant) or to apply Cramer's rule.
examples: =MDETERM(A1:D4) returns the determinant of the 4×4 matrix.

median.description: Returns the middle value of a sorted dataset - half the values are above it and half below. Use it as a more outlier-resistant measure of center than the average.

args: 1-based position of the first character to extract
mid.description: Returns a specified number of characters from a text string, starting at a given position. Use it to extract a substring from the middle of text - pulling the area code from a phone number or a segment from a fixed-format code.

midb.description: Like MID but counts positions and lengths in bytes rather than characters. Used for double-byte character processing in East Asian locales.
examples: =MIDB(A2, 3, 4) returns 4 bytes starting at byte position 3.

min.description: Returns the smallest value in a set of numbers. Use it to find the minimum - lowest score, earliest date, smallest quantity - in a list or range.

args: First value or range; text counts as 0, TRUE as 1, FALSE as 0
mina.description: Returns the smallest value in a range, treating text as 0 and FALSE as 0. The counterpart of MAXA - use it when non-numeric entries should be considered in the minimum calculation.
examples: =MINA(A2:A20) returns the minimum including any text (as 0) or FALSE values.

args: Cells to find the minimum in
Additional criteria for the extra ranges
minifs.description: Returns the minimum value among cells that meet one or more conditions. Use it to find the lowest value within a filtered subset - the smallest order, earliest date within a category.
examples: =MINIFS(C2:C100, B2:B100, "West") returns the smallest value in C where B is "West".

args: Time or date-time value from which to extract the minute (0-59)
minute.description: Extracts the minute component (0–59) from a time value. Use it whenever you need the minute portion of a timestamp - for instance, to check whether a meeting started on time.

minverse.description: Returns the inverse of a square matrix. Use it in linear algebra to solve systems of equations, since multiplying a matrix by its inverse yields the identity matrix. Enter as an array formula over a range the same size as the input.

args: Cash flow amounts; negative values are outflows, positive are inflows
Cost of capital applied to the cash outflows
Rate of return assumed for reinvested positive cash flows
mirr.description: Returns the Modified Internal Rate of Return, which accounts for different financing and reinvestment rates. Use it when the standard IRR assumption - that cash flows are reinvested at the IRR - is unrealistic.

args: First matrix, with m rows and n columns
Second matrix, with n rows and p columns
mmult.description: Multiplies two matrices and returns the result matrix. Use it for matrix arithmetic in linear algebra, portfolio analysis, or any application requiring matrix multiplication. Enter as an array formula over a range sized rows(array1) × cols(array2).

args: Divisor; the result carries the same sign as the divisor
mod.description: Returns the remainder after dividing one number by another. Use it to detect multiples (MOD(n, 3)=0 means n is divisible by 3), alternate row patterns (odd/even), or wrap a numeric sequence.

mode.description: Compatibility name for MODE.SNGL. Prefer MODE.SNGL in new formulas.

mode.mult.description: Returns all values that appear most frequently in a dataset as a vertical array. Use instead of MODE.SNGL when there may be multiple modes.
examples: =MODE.MULT(A2:A20) returns an array of all tied-most-frequent values.

mode.sngl.description: Returns the single most frequently occurring value in a dataset. If there are multiple modes, returns the smallest.
examples: =MODE.SNGL(A2:A20) returns the value that appears most often.

args: Date value from which to extract the month number (1-12)
month.description: Extracts the month number (1–12) from a date. Use it to group data by month, build pivot helper columns, or test whether a date falls in a particular month.
examples: =MONTH(A2) returns 3 for any date in March.

args: Multiple to round to; must have the same sign as number
mround.description: Rounds a number to the nearest multiple of a specified value. Use it to round prices to the nearest nickel, booking times to the nearest 15 minutes, or quantities to the nearest dozen.

multinomial.description: Returns the multinomial coefficient - the number of ways to partition a set into ordered groups of given sizes. Used in combinatorics and probability theory for counting problems with multiple groups.

args: Size of the square identity matrix to generate (n × n)
munit.description: Returns the unit (identity) matrix of a given dimension - a square matrix with 1s on the diagonal and 0s elsewhere. Use it as a starting point for matrix operations or to build identity-based transformations.

args: Value to convert: numbers pass through, TRUE becomes 1, dates become their serial number, everything else becomes 0
n.description: Converts a value to a number: TRUE becomes 1, FALSE becomes 0, text becomes 0, and a number is unchanged. Use it to coerce logical or text values into numbers when a formula requires numeric input.

na.description: Returns the #N/A error value deliberately. Use it to mark missing or unavailable data in a range so that charts skip the gap and lookups handle it explicitly rather than treating it as zero.
examples: =IF(A2="", NA(), A2) inserts #N/A wherever A2 is blank.

negbinom.dist.description: Returns the negative binomial distribution probability - the probability of exactly k failures before achieving the r-th success. Use it in reliability testing and sequential sampling scenarios.
examples: =NEGBINOM.DIST(5, 3, 0.5, FALSE) returns the probability of 5 failures before the 3rd success.
