args: Probability of success on each trial
negbinomdist.description: Compatibility name for NEGBINOM.DIST (non-cumulative form). Prefer NEGBINOM.DIST in new formulas.

args: Optional list of dates to exclude as non-working days
networkdays.description: Counts the number of working days (Monday–Friday) between two dates, optionally excluding a list of holidays. Use it to calculate how many business days remain until a deadline or between two events.
examples: =NETWORKDAYS(A2, B2, Holidays) returns the count of business days, skipping dates listed in the Holidays range.

networkdaysintl.description: Like NETWORKDAYS, but lets you define which days of the week are the weekend using a mask or preset code. Use it for international teams where the weekend might be Friday–Saturday or another combination.
examples: =NETWORKDAYS.INTL(A2, B2, 7, Holidays) treats Friday and Saturday as the weekend.

args: Effective annual interest rate as a decimal
Number of compounding periods per year
nominal.description: Returns the nominal annual interest rate given an effective rate and the number of compounding periods per year. The reverse of EFFECT - use it to back-calculate the stated rate from an observed effective rate.
examples: =NOMINAL(0.0512, 12) returns the nominal rate with monthly compounding.

args: TRUE for the cumulative distribution function; FALSE for the probability density
norm.dist.description: Returns the normal distribution probability (CDF or PDF) for a given mean and standard deviation. Use it to find how likely a value is under a normal distribution - for instance, what fraction of products fall within spec.
examples: =NORM.DIST(75, 70, 10, TRUE) returns P(X ≤ 75) for a N(70, 10) distribution.

norm.inv.description: Returns the inverse of the normal CDF - the value at a given cumulative probability. Use it to find percentile values for any normal distribution.
examples: =NORM.INV(0.95, 70, 10) returns the 95th percentile of a N(70, 10) distribution.

args: TRUE for the cumulative distribution; FALSE for the probability density
norm.s.dist.description: Returns the standard normal distribution probability (mean=0, standard deviation=1). Use it for z-table lookups - finding the probability that a standard normal variable is at most z.

norm.s.inv.description: Returns the inverse of the standard normal CDF - the z-score for a given cumulative probability. Use it to find critical z-values for confidence intervals and hypothesis tests.

args: TRUE for cumulative distribution; FALSE for probability density
normdist.description: Compatibility name for NORM.DIST. Prefer NORM.DIST in new formulas.

norminv.description: Compatibility name for NORM.INV. Prefer NORM.INV in new formulas.

normsdist.description: Compatibility name for NORM.S.DIST (cumulative form). Prefer NORM.S.DIST in new formulas.

normsinv.description: Compatibility name for NORM.S.INV. Prefer NORM.S.INV in new formulas.

not.description: Reverses a logical value - TRUE becomes FALSE and FALSE becomes TRUE.
examples: =NOT(ISBLANK(A2)) returns TRUE when A2 has content.

args: Optional IANA timezone name to return the current time in that zone instead of the local time
now.description: Returns the current date and time as a date-time serial number, recalculating on every worksheet change.
examples: =NOW()-A2 shows how many days (and fraction of a day) have passed since the date in A2.

args: Desired future value after the last payment; defaults to 0
nper.description: Returns the number of periods required to pay off a loan given a constant payment and interest rate. Use it to answer "how many months until this loan is paid off?" for any fixed-rate loan.
examples: =NPER(rate, pmt, pv) returns the number of payment periods.

args: Additional cash flows in chronological order
npv.description: Calculates the Net Present Value of a series of future cash flows discounted at a given rate. Use it to evaluate whether an investment creates value - if NPV is positive, the project earns more than the required rate. Note that the first cash flow is assumed to occur one period from now.

args: Number represented as a text string
Character used as the decimal point; defaults to the locale setting
Character used as the thousands separator
numbervalue.description: Converts a number stored as text to an actual number, letting you specify the decimal and thousands separators. Use it when importing data from locales that use commas as decimal separators.

args: Octal string to convert (up to 10 digits)
Minimum width of the result, padded with leading zeros
oct2bin.description: Converts an octal number to binary. Use it in computing contexts where you need to inspect the bits of an octal value.

args: Octal string to convert to decimal
oct2dec.description: Converts an octal number to decimal. Use it when working with Unix permissions (e.g., "755") or other octal-notated values that you need to calculate with.

args: Minimum width of the result, padded with leading zeros
oct2hex.description: Converts an octal number to hexadecimal. A utility for moving between number systems when both appear in the same data.

args: Value to round up to the nearest odd integer away from zero
odd.description: Rounds a number up to the nearest odd integer. Use it when you need to ensure a value is odd - for example, certain packaging, layout, or algorithm requirements.

args: Rows to move down; negative moves up
Columns to move right; negative moves left
Height of the returned range in rows; defaults to the height of reference
Width of the returned range in columns; defaults to the width of reference
offset.description: Returns a reference shifted a specified number of rows and columns from a starting cell, with an optional width and height. Use it to build dynamic ranges whose size or position changes based on inputs - for instance, a rolling 12-month window.
examples: =SUM(OFFSET(A1, 0, 0, 12, 1)) sums a 12-row column starting at A1.

args: Additional conditions; returns TRUE if any is TRUE
or.description: Returns TRUE if any of its arguments evaluates to TRUE, FALSE only if all are FALSE. Use it to check whether at least one of several conditions holds - for example, flagging rows that belong to any of a set of categories.
examples: =OR(A2="East", A2="West") returns TRUE for either region.

pduration.description: Returns the number of periods required for an investment to reach a target value at a given interest rate. Use it to answer "how long will it take for my investment to double?" at a given return rate.
examples: =PDURATION(rate, pv, fv) returns the number of periods needed.

args: Second dataset, same size as array1
pearson.description: Returns the Pearson product-moment correlation coefficient between two datasets. Functionally identical to CORREL - use either one to measure the linear relationship between two variables.

percentile.description: Compatibility name for PERCENTILE.INC. Prefer PERCENTILE.INC in new formulas.

percentile.exc.description: Returns the k-th percentile of a dataset, excluding the 0th and 100th percentiles. More conservative than PERCENTILE.INC for boundary values.

percentile.inc.description: Returns the k-th percentile of a dataset, including the 0th (minimum) and 100th (maximum) percentiles.

args: Decimal places in the result; defaults to 3
percentrank.description: Compatibility name for PERCENTRANK.INC. Prefer PERCENTRANK.INC in new formulas.

args: Number of decimal places in the result; defaults to 3
percentrank.exc.description: Returns the rank of a value as a percentage of the dataset, excluding 0% and 100%.
examples: =PERCENTRANK.EXC(A2:A20, 75) returns the percentile rank of 75 within the range.

args: Number of decimal places in the result; defaults to 3
percentrank.inc.description: Returns the rank of a value as a percentage (0% to 100%) of the dataset.

args: Number of objects selected, where order matters
permut.description: Returns the number of ordered arrangements (permutations) of a given number of objects chosen from a set, without repetition.
examples: =PERMUT(10, 3) returns 720, the number of ways to arrange 3 items from 10.

args: Number of objects to select, where order matters and repetition is allowed
permutationa.description: Returns the number of ordered arrangements with repetition allowed — each object can be chosen more than once.
examples: =PERMUTATIONA(3, 2) returns 9, because each of 2 positions can hold any of 3 values.

args: Value at which to evaluate the standard normal probability density function
phi.description: Returns the value of the standard normal probability density function at a given z-value. Use it in mathematical and statistical derivations where the normal PDF is needed directly.
examples: =PHI(0) returns approximately 0.3989 (the peak of the bell curve).

pi.description: Returns the mathematical constant π (approximately 3.14159265358979). Use it in any formula involving circles, angles, or trigonometry where π is needed.
examples: =PI()*r^2 computes the area of a circle with radius r.

args: Desired balance after the last payment; defaults to 0
pmt.description: Calculates the fixed periodic payment required to fully amortize a loan at a constant interest rate. Use it to determine monthly mortgage or car loan payments before committing to a loan.
examples: =PMT(5%/12, 360, -200000) returns the monthly payment for a $200,000 mortgage at 5% over 30 years.

args: TRUE for cumulative distribution; FALSE for exact probability
poisson.description: Compatibility name for POISSON.DIST. Prefer POISSON.DIST in new formulas.

poisson.dist.description: Returns the Poisson distribution probability for a given number of events and average rate. Use it to model the probability of a count of rare, independent events - customer arrivals, server requests, or defects per unit.
examples: =POISSON.DIST(3, 5, FALSE) returns P(X=3) when the average is 5 events.

args: Exponent to raise the base to
power.description: Returns a number raised to a given power - equivalent to the ^ operator but sometimes clearer in complex formulas. Use it for any exponentiation: squaring values, computing compound growth, or applying a known power law.

args: Period number to calculate the principal portion for (1-based)
Desired balance after the last payment; defaults to 0
ppmt.description: Returns the principal portion of a specific loan payment. Use it alongside IPMT to understand how much of a given payment reduces the outstanding balance.
examples: =PPMT(rate, period, nper, pv) returns the principal for that period.

pricedisc.description: Returns the price per $100 face value of a discounted security (one that pays no coupon and is sold below its redemption value).
examples: =PRICEDISC(A2, B2, 0.04, 100, 0) returns the price of a T-bill with a 4% discount rate.

args: Annual coupon rate as a decimal
pricemat.description: Returns the price per $100 face value of a security that pays interest only at maturity rather than periodically.
examples: =PRICEMAT(A2, B2, C2, 0.05, 0.04, 0) prices a maturity-interest security.

args: Probabilities corresponding to each value in x_range
Lower bound of the range to sum probabilities for
Upper bound; if omitted, returns the probability that x equals lower_limit
prob.description: Returns the probability that a value in x_range falls within a specified interval, given a set of discrete probability weights.
examples: =PROB(A2:A6, B2:B6, 2, 4) returns the total probability of values between 2 and 4.

args: First value or range to multiply
product.description: Multiplies all numbers in a list together and returns the result. Use it to find the product of a range - for instance, computing compound growth over multiple periods by multiplying a series of growth factors.
examples: =PRODUCT(A2:A10) multiplies all values in the range.

args: String to convert to title case (first letter of each word capitalised)
proper.description: Capitalizes the first letter of each word in a text string and lowercases the rest. Use it to standardize names and titles that arrived in inconsistent case - all-caps, all-lowercase, or random mixed case.

args: Future value after the last payment; defaults to 0
pv.description: Returns the present value of a series of future payments discounted at a constant rate. Use it to answer "what is a stream of future payments worth today?" - for loan valuations, annuity pricing, or lease analysis.
examples: =PV(rate, nper, pmt) returns the lump-sum equivalent value today.

quartile.description: Compatibility name for QUARTILE.INC. Prefer QUARTILE.INC in new formulas.

quartile.exc.description: Returns the specified quartile of a dataset, excluding the 0th and 4th quartiles (min and max).
examples: =QUARTILE.EXC(A2:A20, 1) returns the first quartile (25th percentile, exclusive).

quartile.inc.description: Returns the specified quartile of a dataset, including min (0) and max (4).

quotient.description: Returns the integer portion of a division result, discarding the remainder. Use it when you only want the whole-number quotient without dealing with decimals.

args: Angle in degrees to convert to radians
radians.description: Converts an angle from degrees to radians. Use it before passing degree values to trigonometric functions (SIN, COS, TAN), which expect radians.

rand.description: Returns a random number between 0 (inclusive) and 1 (exclusive), recalculated on every worksheet change. Use it to generate random values for simulations or random sampling.
examples: =INT(RAND()*100)+1 returns a random integer from 1 to 100.

args: Number of rows in the output array
TRUE to return integers; FALSE for decimals (default)
randarray.description: Returns a two-dimensional array of random numbers between specified minimum and maximum values. Use it to fill a range with random data for simulations or testing - all values generated at once with a single formula.
examples: =RANDARRAY(5, 3, 1, 100, TRUE) fills a 5×3 block with random integers from 1 to 100.

args: Smallest integer that can be returned
Largest integer that can be returned
randbetween.description: Returns a random integer between two specified values (inclusive), recalculated on every change. Use it to simulate dice rolls, random assignment, or integer random sampling.

args: List of numbers to rank against
rank.description: Compatibility name for RANK.EQ. Prefer RANK.EQ in new formulas.

args: List of numbers to rank against
0 or omitted ranks in descending order; any other value ranks ascending
rank.avg.description: Returns the rank of a number within a list, averaging the rank when ties occur. Use it for fair ranking where you want tied values to share the average of their positions rather than one getting an arbitrary advantage.
examples: =RANK.AVG(A2, A$2:A$100, 0) returns A2's rank in descending order.

args: List of numbers to rank against
0 or omitted ranks descending; any other value ranks ascending
rank.eq.description: Returns the rank of a number within a list; all tied values receive the top rank in the group. Use it for traditional ranking where tied values claim the same (highest) position in the tie.

args: Future value after the last payment; defaults to 0
Starting estimate for the rate; defaults to 0.1
rate.description: Calculates the periodic interest rate of an annuity given the number of periods, payment amount, and present value. Use it to determine the implied interest rate on a loan when you know the payment schedule.
examples: =RATE(nper, pmt, pv) returns the per-period rate; multiply by 12 for an annual figure.

received.description: Returns the total amount received at maturity for a fully invested security — the original investment grown by the discount rate over the holding period.
examples: =RECEIVED(A2, B2, 1000, 0.04, 0) returns what you collect when the security matures.

args: Array or range to iterate over
LAMBDA(accumulator, value) that returns the new accumulator; the final accumulator is the result
reduce.description: Applies a LAMBDA iteratively over an array to accumulate a single result, returning only the final value. Use it to build custom aggregations - like summing only values above a threshold - that no built-in aggregate handles.
examples: =REDUCE(0, A2:A10, LAMBDA(acc, x, IF(x>100, acc+x, acc))) sums only values over 100.

args: Which occurrence to return when return_mode is 0
regexextract.description: Extracts the first substring that matches a regular expression pattern from a text string. Use it to pull structured data out of free-form text - phone numbers, postal codes, dates - without complex nested formulas.
examples: =REGEXEXTRACT(A2, "\d{5}") returns the first 5-digit sequence in A2.

args: Replacement string; use \1, \2 etc. for capture group references
Which match to replace; 0 or omitted replaces all
regexreplace.description: Replaces substrings that match a regular expression pattern with a replacement string. Use it for advanced find-and-replace operations that plain SUBSTITUTE can't handle - removing all punctuation, replacing multiple patterns at once, or manipulating structured text.

args: Regular expression pattern to test against
regextest.description: Returns TRUE if a text string contains a match for a regular expression pattern. Use it to validate data formats - checking whether a cell holds a valid email, phone number, or date pattern - without extracting the value.
examples: =REGEXTEST(A2, "^\d{5}(-\d{4})?$") returns TRUE for a valid US ZIP code.

args: 1-based position of the first character to replace
Number of characters to remove from old_text
String to insert in place of the removed characters
replace.description: Replaces a specified number of characters at a given position in a text string with a new string. Use it when you know the exact position of what you want to replace - updating a fixed-format code field or inserting a character at a known offset.

replaceb.description: Like REPLACE but counts positions and lengths in bytes. Used for double-byte character processing in East Asian locales.

args: Number of times to repeat the string
rept.description: Repeats a text string a specified number of times. Use it to build simple in-cell bar charts, pad strings to a fixed width, or create repeated separator patterns inside a formula.
examples: =REPT("|", A2) creates a bar whose length matches the value in A2.

args: Number of characters to extract from the end; defaults to 1
right.description: Returns the specified number of characters from the end of a text string. Use it to extract a suffix - the last four digits of an account number, a file extension, or a trailing code.

rightb.description: Like RIGHT but counts characters in bytes. Used in East Asian locale text processing.

args: Positive integer to convert to a Roman numeral
roman.description: Converts an Arabic number to Roman numeral notation, returned as text. Use it to format numbers in classical style for titles, outlines, or documents where Roman numerals are conventional.

args: Decimal places to round to; negative rounds left of the decimal point
round.description: Rounds a number to a specified number of decimal places, using standard rounding (≥0.5 rounds up). Use it to control precision in financial totals, measurements, or any calculated value shown to users.

args: Decimal places to keep; negative rounds left of the decimal point
rounddown.description: Rounds a number toward zero to a specified number of decimal places - always rounding down regardless of the digit that follows. Use it when you always want the more conservative value.

args: Value to round away from zero
Decimal places to keep; negative rounds left of the decimal point
roundup.description: Rounds a number away from zero to a specified number of decimal places - always rounding up regardless of the digit that follows. Use it when you always want to round conservatively upward.

args: Cell or range whose row number to return; uses the formula cell if omitted
row.description: Returns the row number of a reference (or the current cell if no reference is given). Use it to generate row-number sequences, add a row-number column, or control array formula behavior.

args: Range or array whose row count you want
rows.description: Returns the number of rows in a range or array. Use it to find the height of a dynamic range, or when you need to loop over all rows in a computed result.

rri.description: Returns the equivalent interest rate needed to grow an investment from a present value to a future value over a number of periods. Use it to calculate a Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR).
examples: =RRI(5, 100, 150) returns the per-period growth rate over 5 periods.

rsq.description: Returns the square of the Pearson correlation coefficient (R²) - the proportion of variance in y explained by x. Use it to evaluate how well a linear regression fits your data; R²=1 means a perfect fit.
examples: =RSQ(B2:B20, A2:A20) returns R² for the linear relationship.

args: Array or range to iterate over
LAMBDA(accumulator, value) that returns the new accumulator; each intermediate accumulator is collected into the output array
scan.description: Like REDUCE, but returns an array showing the accumulated value after each step rather than just the final result. Use it to create running totals or cumulative calculations where you want to see every intermediate value.
examples: =SCAN(0, A2:A10, LAMBDA(acc, x, acc+x)) returns the running sum at each row.

args: Substring to locate; supports the wildcards ? and *
1-based character position to start from; defaults to 1
search.description: Returns the position of one text string within another, case-insensitively, with support for the wildcards ? (any single character) and * (any sequence). Use it for flexible substring searches when the exact capitalization of the target may vary.

searchb.description: Like SEARCH but counts positions in bytes for East Asian locales.
examples: =SEARCHB("A", A2) returns the byte position of the first case-insensitive match of "A".

sec.description: Returns the secant of an angle in radians (1 divided by cosine). A trigonometric function used in geometry and physics.

sech.description: Returns the hyperbolic secant of a number. Used in advanced mathematical and physical analysis.

args: Time or date-time value from which to extract the seconds (0-59)
second.description: Extracts the seconds component (0–59) from a time value. Useful for precise time analysis - for example, checking whether a process completed within an expected number of seconds.

args: Number of rows in the output array
First value in the sequence; defaults to 1
Step between consecutive values; defaults to 1
sequence.description: Generates a two-dimensional array of sequential numbers with a specified start and step. Use it to fill a range with a number series without dragging or entering values manually.

seriessum.description: Returns the sum of a power series: Σ aᵢ × x^(n+m×i). Use it in engineering and mathematics to evaluate polynomial approximations of functions.
examples: =SERIESSUM(x, 0, 1, coefficients) evaluates the polynomial at x using the given coefficient array.

args: Sheet name or reference whose index to return; returns the current sheet index if omitted
sheet.description: Returns the sheet number of a reference or the current sheet if no argument is given. Use it to identify which sheet a reference belongs to or to build formulas that react to their position in the workbook.
examples: =SHEET() returns the index number of the current sheet.

args: Reference whose sheet count to return; returns the total workbook sheet count if omitted
sheets.description: Returns the total number of sheets in a workbook, or the number of sheets covered by a 3D reference. Use it to build dynamic formulas that adapt when sheets are added or removed.
examples: =SHEETS() returns how many sheets the workbook contains.

args: Value whose sign to return: -1 for negative, 0 for zero, 1 for positive
sign.description: Returns 1 if a number is positive, −1 if negative, and 0 if zero. Use it to determine the direction of a value without caring about its magnitude - for instance, to check whether a change is an increase or decrease.

sin.description: Returns the sine of an angle given in radians. Core trigonometric function used in geometry, physics, signal processing, and engineering.

sinh.description: Returns the hyperbolic sine of a number. Used in engineering and physics - for instance, in calculations involving hanging cables (catenary) and heat transfer.

skew.description: Returns the skewness of a distribution - a measure of asymmetry around the mean. Positive skew means a longer right tail; negative means a longer left tail. Use it to check whether data is approximately symmetric before applying methods that assume normality.

args: First value or range (treats data as the whole population)
skew.p.description: Returns the population skewness, dividing by n instead of n−1. Use it when your data represents the entire population rather than a sample.

args: Residual value at the end of its useful life
Number of periods in the asset's useful life
sln.description: Returns the straight-line depreciation of an asset for one period. Use it when accounting rules require equal depreciation charges every year over the asset's useful life.
examples: =SLN(10000, 1000, 5) returns $1,800 per year for a $10,000 asset with $1,000 salvage value and a 5-year life.

args: Independent (x) data points, same count as known_ys
slope.description: Returns the slope of the linear regression line through a set of data points - how much y changes for each unit increase in x. Use it with INTERCEPT to build a simple linear model for prediction or trend analysis.

args: Rank from the bottom (1 returns the minimum)
small.description: Returns the k-th smallest value in a dataset. Use it to find bottom-N values - the second-lowest, third-cheapest - without sorting the data.

args: Column (or row) index to sort by; defaults to 1
TRUE to sort by row instead of by column
sort.description: Returns a sorted copy of an array based on one or more columns, in ascending or descending order. Use it to present data in order without disturbing the source - the result spills automatically and updates as data changes.
examples: =SORT(A2:B20, 2, -1) sorts by column B in descending order.

args: Array to sort on (same height as array for row sort)
sortby.description: Sorts an array by the values in one or more external arrays rather than by its own columns. Use it to sort one list based on the order defined in a separate related list.
examples: =SORTBY(A2:A10, B2:B10) sorts the names in A2:A10 by the scores in B2:B10.

args: Non-negative value whose square root you want
sqrt.description: Returns the positive square root of a number. Use it whenever square roots appear in formulas - distance calculations, statistical formulas, geometry.

args: Non-negative multiplier of π inside the square root
sqrtpi.description: Returns the square root of (number × π). Useful in probability and statistics - particularly for formulas derived from the normal distribution.

standardize.description: Returns the standardized (z-score) equivalent of a value: (x − mean) / standard_deviation. Use it to convert raw values to standard deviations from the mean for comparison across different scales or distributions.

args: First value or range (treated as a sample)
stdev.description: Estimates standard deviation based on a sample, ignoring text and logical values. Equivalent to STDEV.S.
examples: =STDEV(A2:A20) returns the sample standard deviation of the range.

args: First value or range (treats data as the whole population)
stdev.p.description: Returns the population standard deviation (divides by n). Use it when your values represent the entire population and you want the exact spread of that population.

args: First value or range (treats data as a sample)
stdev.s.description: Returns the sample standard deviation (divides by n−1). The most common standard deviation function - use it when your data is a sample from a larger population and you want an unbiased estimate of the population spread.

args: First value or range; text and FALSE count as 0, TRUE as 1
stdeva.description: Like STDEV.S but includes text (as 0) and logical values in the calculation. Use it when your range has mixed types and non-numeric entries should contribute to the spread.

args: First value or range (treated as the whole population)
stdevp.description: Compatibility name for STDEV.P. Prefer STDEV.P in new formulas.

args: First value or range; text and FALSE count as 0, TRUE as 1 (whole population)
stdevpa.description: Like STDEV.P but includes text and logical values. Use it for population standard deviation over a mixed-type range.

steyx.description: Returns the standard error of the predicted y values in a linear regression - a measure of how accurate the regression line's predictions are on average. Use it to gauge the precision of your linear model.
examples: =STEYX(B2:B20, A2:A20) returns the standard error of the estimate.

args: Which occurrence to replace; replaces all occurrences if omitted
substitute.description: Replaces all occurrences (or a specific occurrence) of a substring within a text string. Use it for formula-driven find-and-replace when you don't know the position - replacing spaces with underscores, removing unwanted characters, or swapping out a code value.

args: Function code: 1-11 include manually hidden rows; 101-111 exclude them (e.g. 9 or 109 = SUM, 1 or 101 = AVERAGE)
subtotal.description: Returns a subtotal of a list using any of 11 aggregation functions (SUM, AVERAGE, COUNT, etc.), and can ignore hidden rows. Use it in filtered tables so that your aggregate updates to reflect only the visible data - unlike SUM, which always includes hidden rows.

args: First value or range to add
sum.description: Adds all numbers in a range together and returns the total. The most-used function in Excel - use it to total a column of sales, sum expenses, or add up any list of numbers.
examples: =SUM(A2:A100) returns the total of the entire range.

args: Cells to evaluate against the condition
Cells to sum; defaults to range if omitted
sumif.description: Adds values in a range that meet a single condition. Use it to total a specific category - for instance, summing sales for the "West" region only, or summing amounts above a threshold.
examples: =SUMIF(B2:B100, "West", C2:C100) sums C where B is "West".

sumifs.description: Adds values in a range that meet multiple conditions simultaneously. Use it like SUMIF but with several filters at once - for example, sales in the West region for a specific product and month.
examples: =SUMIFS(C2:C100, B2:B100, "West", D2:D100, "Widgets") sums C where both conditions are met.

args: Additional arrays to multiply element-wise before summing; all must be the same size
sumproduct.description: Multiplies corresponding elements in two or more arrays, then sums all the products. Use it for weighted averages, multi-criteria conditional sums without SUMIFS, or any calculation that involves pairing and aggregating two lists.

sumsq.description: Returns the sum of the squares of its arguments. Use it in statistics and engineering - for instance, when computing the sum of squared errors or the squared magnitude of a vector.

args: Y values, same count as array_x
sumx2my2.description: Returns the sum of differences of squares of corresponding values in two arrays: Σ(x²−y²). Used in specialized mathematical and physics calculations.

args: Y values, same count as array_x
sumx2py2.description: Returns the sum of sums of squares of corresponding values in two arrays: Σ(x²+y²). Useful for computing the sum of squared magnitudes of paired values.

args: Y values, same count as array_x
sumxmy2.description: Returns the sum of squared differences of corresponding values in two arrays: Σ(x−y)². Use it to compute the total squared error between two sets of values - for example, between predictions and actuals.

args: Value to compare against each case
First value to match and its result (passed as consecutive arguments)
Additional pairs; if the last argument has no matching value, it is treated as the default
switch.description: Compares an expression against a list of values and returns the result paired with the first match. Use it instead of nested IFs when you are matching a single value against several exact cases - like translating a status code into a readable label.

args: Residual value at end of useful life
Number of periods in the asset's useful life
syd.description: Returns the sum-of-years'-digits depreciation for a specific period - a method that allocates more depreciation to early years and less to later ones. Use it when accelerated depreciation is required but double-declining balance is too aggressive.

args: Expression to test; returns the text if it is a string, otherwise an empty string
t.description: Returns the text value in a cell, or an empty string if the cell doesn't contain text. Use it as a type gate to pass only text values through a formula while silently converting numbers and other types to an empty string.
examples: =T(A2) returns A2's text if it is text, otherwise "".

t.dist.description: Returns the Student's t-distribution probability (left-tailed CDF or PDF). Use it in hypothesis testing for small samples when the population standard deviation is unknown.
examples: =T.DIST(2.0, 10, TRUE) returns the left-tail probability with 10 degrees of freedom.

t.dist.2t.description: Returns the two-tailed t-distribution probability for a given t-statistic. Use it for two-tailed t-tests - when you're testing whether a difference is non-zero in either direction.

t.dist.rt.description: Returns the right-tailed t-distribution probability. Use it for one-tailed t-tests where you're testing whether a value is greater than a null hypothesis value.

t.inv.description: Returns the inverse of the left-tailed t-distribution - the t-value at a given probability. Use it to find critical values for one-tailed tests or to construct confidence intervals.

t.inv.2t.description: Returns the inverse of the two-tailed t-distribution. Use it to find the critical t-value for a two-tailed hypothesis test at a given significance level.
examples: =T.INV.2T(0.05, 10) returns the critical value for a 5% two-tailed test with 10 degrees of freedom.

t.test.description: Returns the p-value for a Student's t-test comparing two datasets. Use it to determine whether the difference in means between two groups is statistically significant.
examples: =T.TEST(A2:A20, B2:B20, 2, 2) performs a two-tailed, two-sample t-test.

args: Rows to take from the top; negative takes from the bottom
Columns to take from the left; negative takes from the right
take.description: Returns a specified number of rows or columns from the start or end of an array. Use it to grab the top N or bottom N rows from a dataset - for instance, to show the five most recent entries.
examples: =TAKE(A2:D100, 5) returns the first 5 rows; =TAKE(A2:D100, -5) returns the last 5.

tan.description: Returns the tangent of an angle given in radians. Use it in geometry and physics for slope calculations, angle problems, and wave analysis.

tanh.description: Returns the hyperbolic tangent of a number, always between −1 and 1. Used in engineering, statistics, and as a smooth sigmoid-like function in mathematical models.

tbilleq.description: Returns the bond-equivalent yield of a Treasury bill. Use it to compare a T-bill's return to coupon-bearing bonds on a consistent annual-yield basis.
examples: =TBILLEQ(settlement, maturity, discount) returns the equivalent annual yield.

tbillprice.description: Returns the price per $100 face value of a Treasury bill. Use it to calculate what you pay today for a T-bill given its discount rate and dates.

tbillyield.description: Returns the yield of a Treasury bill given its price and dates. Use it to find the return on a T-bill you already hold or are evaluating.

tdist.description: Compatibility name for T.DIST.RT or T.DIST.2T. Prefer T.DIST.RT or T.DIST.2T in new formulas.

args: Numeric value or date to format
Excel format code that controls the output, e.g. "0.00" or "MMM YYYY"
text.description: Formats a number as text using a custom number-format code. Use it to embed a formatted number inside a text string - for example, to display a date or currency amount with the formatting intact when concatenating with other text.

args: Which occurrence to use; negative counts from the end
TRUE treats the end of the string as an implicit delimiter
Value to return when the delimiter is not found
textafter.description: Returns the text that occurs after a specified delimiter, with options to select which occurrence and how to handle a missing match. Use it to split text without knowing the character position - extracting the domain from an email address, for instance.

args: Which occurrence to use; negative counts from the end
TRUE treats the start of the string as an implicit delimiter
Value to return when the delimiter is not found
textbefore.description: Returns the text that occurs before a specified delimiter. The counterpart of TEXTAFTER - use it to extract the portion of a string that comes before a known separator.
examples: =TEXTBEFORE(A2, "@") returns the username portion of an email address.

args: Separator to place between each joined value
TRUE to skip empty cells; FALSE to include them as empty strings
First value or range to join
textjoin.description: Joins a range of text values with a specified delimiter, optionally skipping blank cells. Use it to build a comma-separated list, a readable sentence, or any delimited string from a column or row of values.
examples: =TEXTJOIN(", ", TRUE, A2:A10) produces "Alice, Bob, Carol" (skipping any blanks).

args: Character (or array of characters) that separates columns
Character that separates rows; omit for a single-row result
TRUE to treat consecutive delimiters as one
Pad value used to fill in shorter rows; defaults to #N/A
textsplit.description: Splits a text string into a two-dimensional array using row and/or column delimiters. Use it to parse delimited data - splitting a CSV line into separate columns or a multi-line block into rows - in a single formula that spills automatically.
examples: =TEXTSPLIT(A2, ",") splits comma-separated values into individual cells.

time.description: Creates a time value from separate hour, minute, and second numbers. Use it to assemble a time from components or to add a precise time increment to a date.

args: Time expressed as a recognisable text string, e.g. "2:30 PM"
timevalue.description: Converts a time stored as text (such as "2:30 PM") into a decimal fraction that Excel can calculate with. Use it when time values arrived as text strings after an import.
examples: =TIMEVALUE("14:30:00") returns 0.6042, the fraction of a 24-hour day.

tinv.description: Compatibility name for T.INV.2T. Prefer T.INV.2T in new formulas.

args: Array or range to collapse into a single column
TRUE scans by row (left to right, top to bottom); FALSE scans by column (default)
tocol.description: Converts an array or range into a single column, optionally ignoring blanks or errors. Use it to flatten a two-dimensional table into a vertical list for further processing or deduplication.
examples: =TOCOL(A1:D5) stacks all 20 values into one column.

today.description: Returns today's date (with no time component) and updates on every recalculation. Use it for age calculations, days-until-deadline formulas, or any formula that should always reflect the current date.
examples: =TODAY()-A2 gives the number of days since the date in A2.

args: Array or range to collapse into a single row
TRUE scans by column; FALSE scans by row (default)
torow.description: Converts an array or range into a single row, optionally ignoring blanks or errors. Use it to flatten a table into a horizontal list.
examples: =TOROW(A1:D5) returns all 20 values in a single row.

args: Array or range to flip so rows become columns and vice versa
transpose.description: Rotates an array so that rows become columns and columns become rows. Use it to reorient data - for example, converting a vertical list of months into a horizontal header row, or vice versa.

args: New x values for which to predict y
TRUE (default) fits the intercept; FALSE forces it to zero
trend.description: Returns predicted y-values along a linear trend fitted to existing data. The linear counterpart to GROWTH.
examples: =TREND(B2:B6, A2:A6, A7:A10) predicts future values along the fitted line.

args: String to remove leading, trailing, and extra interior spaces from
trim.description: Removes leading and trailing spaces from a text string and collapses internal multiple spaces to single spaces. Use it to clean up data entered or imported with extra whitespace, which prevents correct matching, sorting, and lookups.

args: Fraction of data points to exclude from each end (e.g. 0.1 trims 5% from each end)
trimmean.description: Returns the mean of the interior of a dataset after excluding a specified proportion of the most extreme values from both ends.
examples: =TRIMMEAN(A2:A20, 0.2) averages after dropping the top and bottom 10%.

args: TRUE removes blank rows at the top and bottom edges
TRUE removes blank columns at the left and right edges
trimrange.description: Trims rows and/or columns of empty cells from the edges of an array. Use it to remove trailing blanks from a dynamic spill result before passing it to another function that would be thrown off by extra empty rows.
examples: =TRIMRANGE(A1:D20) removes any all-blank rows or columns at the edges.

true.description: Returns the logical value TRUE. Use it in formulas that require a literal TRUE as a function argument - for instance, when specifying cumulative probability in a distribution function.

args: Decimal places to keep; defaults to 0 (integer result)
trunc.description: Removes the fractional part of a number, always rounding toward zero regardless of sign. Use it when you want to drop decimals without rounding - unlike INT, =TRUNC(-4.9) returns -4, not -5.

ttest.description: Compatibility name for T.TEST. Prefer T.TEST in new formulas.

args: Expression whose type code to return: 1=number, 2=text, 4=logical, 8=formula, 16=error, 64=array
type.description: Returns a number indicating the data type of a value: 1 for number, 2 for text, 4 for logical, 16 for error, 64 for array. Use it to write type-safe formulas or to diagnose unexpected data types in a cell.

args: Unicode code point to convert to its character
unichar.description: Returns the Unicode character corresponding to a given code point. Use it to insert any Unicode character - symbols, accented letters, emojis - by their code number when you can't type them directly.

args: String whose first character's Unicode code point you want
unicode.description: Returns the Unicode code point of the first character in a text string. Use it alongside UNICHAR to inspect or transform individual Unicode characters.

args: TRUE to return unique columns; FALSE for unique rows (default)
TRUE to return only values that appear exactly once; FALSE returns all distinct values (default)
unique.description: Returns the distinct values from a list or table, removing duplicates. Use it to build a unique list of items - categories, customers, regions - that updates automatically as new data is added.
examples: =UNIQUE(A2:A100) spills the distinct values found in column A.

upper.description: Converts all letters in a text string to uppercase. Use it to normalize text for display or comparison - ensuring all ticker symbols, country codes, or identifiers are consistently uppercase.

args: Number, date, or time stored as a text string
value.description: Converts a text string that looks like a number into an actual numeric value. Use it when numbers are stored as text - a common problem after imports - causing arithmetic to fail or sort incorrectly.

args: Value to convert to a text representation
0=concise format (default), 1=strict format with quotes and delimiters
valuetotext.description: Converts any value - including arrays, errors, and logical values - to its text representation in a concise or strict format. Use it to display or inspect a value as a string, especially for debugging or generating readable output from dynamic arrays.

args: First value or range (treated as a sample)
var.description: Compatibility name for VAR.S. Prefer VAR.S in new formulas.

args: First value or range (treats data as the whole population)
var.p.description: Returns the population variance (divides by n). Use it when your values are the entire population and you want the average squared deviation from the mean.

args: First value or range (treats data as a sample)
var.s.description: Returns the sample variance (divides by n−1). The most common variance function - use it when your data is a sample from a larger population.

args: First value or range; text and FALSE count as 0, TRUE as 1 (sample variance)
vara.description: Like VAR.S but includes text (as 0) and logical values. Use it when your range has mixed types and non-numeric entries should be counted.

args: First value or range (treated as the whole population)
varp.description: Compatibility name for VAR.P. Prefer VAR.P in new formulas.

args: First value or range; text and FALSE count as 0, TRUE as 1 (population variance)
varpa.description: Like VAR.P but includes text and logical values. Use it for population variance over a mixed-type range.

args: Value to search for in the first column of the table
1-based column number within the table to return a value from
TRUE for approximate match (first column must be sorted ascending); FALSE for exact match
vlookup.description: Searches for a value in the first column of a table and returns a value from another column in the same row. The classic lookup function - use it to join tables on a key, like finding a price for a product code. Note: XLOOKUP is more flexible for complex lookups.
examples: =VLOOKUP(A2, PriceTable, 3, FALSE) returns the value from column 3 of PriceTable where column 1 matches A2.

args: Additional arrays to stack below, matched by column count
vstack.description: Combines multiple arrays or ranges one on top of the other (vertically) into a single array. Use it to concatenate tables that share the same column structure.
examples: =VSTACK(A1:C10, A12:C20) stacks two tables into one continuous block.

args: Date value whose day-of-week number you want
Controls which day is 1: 1=Sun-Sat, 2=Mon-Sun, 3=Mon-Sun starting from 0, and others
weekday.description: Returns a number representing the day of the week for a given date, using a return_type you specify (e.g., 1 = Sunday through 7 = Saturday). Use it to highlight weekends, filter to specific days, or build conditional formatting rules.
examples: =WEEKDAY(A2, 2) returns 1 for Monday and 7 for Sunday.

args: Date whose week number you want
Day the week starts on: 1=Sunday (default), 2=Monday, 21=ISO Monday
weeknum.description: Returns the week number of the year (1–53) for a given date. Use it to group data by week or to calculate how many weeks into the year a date falls.
examples: =WEEKNUM(A2) returns the calendar week number for the date in A2.

args: TRUE for cumulative distribution; FALSE for probability density
weibull.description: Compatibility name for WEIBULL.DIST. Prefer WEIBULL.DIST in new formulas.

args: TRUE for the cumulative distribution; FALSE for the probability density
weibull.dist.description: Returns the Weibull distribution probability (CDF or PDF). Use it to model time-to-failure or reliability data - the Weibull distribution is widely used in engineering for survival and failure analysis.
examples: =WEIBULL.DIST(100, 1.5, 200, TRUE) returns the probability that a component fails by time 100.

args: Working days to advance; negative moves backward
Optional list of additional non-working dates to skip
workday.description: Returns the date that is a specified number of working days before or after a start date, optionally skipping holidays. Use it to calculate delivery deadlines, response-time commitments, or any milestone defined in business days.
examples: =WORKDAY(A2, 10, Holidays) returns the date 10 business days after A2.

workdayintl.description: Like WORKDAY, but lets you define a custom weekend using a mask or preset code. Use it when your organization operates on a non-standard weekly schedule - for instance, a Friday–Saturday weekend.
examples: =WORKDAY.INTL(A2, 10, 7, Holidays) treats Friday and Saturday as the weekend.

args: Maximum number of items per column
Value to pad incomplete columns with; defaults to #N/A
wrapcols.description: Wraps a one-dimensional array into a multi-column layout of a specified row height. Use it to reflow a vertical list into a grid - for instance, turning 12 month names into a 3×4 block.
examples: =WRAPCOLS(A1:A12, 4) creates three columns of four items each.

args: Maximum number of items per row
Value to pad incomplete rows with; defaults to #N/A
wraprows.description: Wraps a one-dimensional array into a multi-row layout of a specified width. The horizontal counterpart to WRAPCOLS - use it to arrange a list into rows of fixed length.
examples: =WRAPROWS(A1:A12, 4) creates three rows of four items each.

args: Cash flow amounts; must include at least one negative and one positive value
Corresponding dates for each cash flow, in any order
xirr.description: Calculates the Internal Rate of Return for a series of cash flows occurring on irregular (non-periodic) dates. Use it for real-world investment analysis where cash flows don't land on regular intervals.

args: Single-row or single-column range to search
Range of the same size to return a value from
Value to return when no match is found; omitting returns #N/A
0=exact (default), -1=exact or next smaller, 1=exact or next larger, 2=wildcard
Search mode: 1=first to last (default), -1=last to first, 2=binary ascending, -2=binary descending
xlookup.description: Searches for a value in a lookup array and returns the corresponding value from a result array, with options for match mode and search direction. Use it as the modern replacement for VLOOKUP and HLOOKUP - it can search in any direction, return multiple columns, and handle missing values gracefully.
examples: =XLOOKUP(A2, B2:B100, C2:D100, "Not found") returns two columns of results where B matches A2.

args: Single-row or single-column range to search
0=exact (default), -1=exact or next smaller, 1=exact or next larger, 2=wildcard
Search mode: 1=first to last (default), -1=last to first, 2=binary ascending, -2=binary descending
xmatch.description: Searches for a value in an array and returns its position, with options for match type and search direction. Use it as a more capable replacement for MATCH - it supports wildcards, binary search, and reverse lookup.
examples: =XMATCH(A2, B2:B100, 0, -1) finds the last exact match position.

args: Corresponding dates for each cash flow
xnpv.description: Returns the Net Present Value of a series of cash flows at irregular dates, discounted at a specified rate. Use it when cash flows occur on specific but non-uniform dates - a more realistic alternative to NPV.

args: Additional conditions; returns TRUE if an odd number of them are TRUE
xor.description: Returns TRUE if an odd number of its arguments are TRUE (exclusive OR). Use it to detect whether exactly one of two conditions is met - for instance, when a record must have a start date or an end date but not both.
examples: =XOR(ISBLANK(A2), ISBLANK(B2)) returns TRUE when exactly one date is missing.

args: Date value from which to extract the four-digit year
year.description: Extracts the four-digit year from a date value. Use it to group data by year, filter records to a specific year, or build year-over-year calculations.
examples: =YEAR(A2) returns 2025 for any date in that year.

yearfrac.description: Returns the fraction of a year represented by the number of days between two dates, using a specified day-count basis. Use it for financial calculations such as accrued interest, partial-year proration, or annualized returns.
examples: =YEARFRAC(A2, B2, 1) returns the actual fraction of a year between the two dates.

yielddisc.description: Returns the annualised yield of a discounted security such as a Treasury bill.
examples: =YIELDDISC(A2, B2, 97, 100, 0) returns the annual yield for a T-bill priced at 97.

args: Annual coupon rate as a decimal
yieldmat.description: Returns the annual yield of a security that pays interest only at maturity.
examples: =YIELDMAT(A2, B2, C2, 0.05, 99, 0) returns the yield of a maturity-interest bond priced at 99.

z.test.description: Returns the one-tailed p-value of a z-test - the probability that the sample mean is at least as extreme as observed, given a hypothesized population mean. Use it to test whether a sample mean is consistent with a known or assumed population mean.
examples: =Z.TEST(A2:A30, 50, 10) tests the sample against a hypothesized mean of 50 with standard deviation 10.

args: Population standard deviation; if omitted, the sample standard deviation is used
ztest.description: Compatibility name for Z.TEST. Prefer Z.TEST in new formulas.
